titre: How to play League of Legends...
transcription: [Music] This is League of Legends and this is my good friend Josh. Josh has never played League of Legends, but after watching Arcane season 2, episode 8, minute 30, 28 seconds in on frame 15, he decided he wanted to try it. So, this video is literally everything you need to know about League of Legends made for Josh. Now, first things first, you got to get the game. Go to the League website, download the game, create an account, make a username, and remember, this username cannot be changed. So, don't do something stupid like your full name or those fun digits on the back of your parents' credit card. Instead, send those digits to me. Once you finish making an account, make sure you read the entire terms of service agreement. I actually did it. Took about an hour and a half of straight reading. Also, there's a typo in there. Vance nose to you. But if if we have Oh, a typo. 40 minutes in and I found a typo. Riot. I mean, what are you doing? I'm so [ __ ] tired. Then you get to pick your game name. Now, this is changeable, so you can name it something stupid like your full name. Then load up the game and do the tutorial. Now, this tutorial will teach you the very bare bones of things, but it's extremely exaggerated. For instance, the damage you deal, the damage you take, the XP you get, the gold you get, and all of that is extremely exaggerated, so it's super super easy. So, I am going to cover some things the tutorial did, but I'm going to do it better. Now, once you're out of the tutorial, you're going to be met with a hodgepodge of menus and labels to click. Don't worry about any of them. Just click play, go to Swift Play, hit ready up, pick your champion, pick your role, and get in. Although, maybe finish this video first. Now, this video is for my friend Josh, but since other people are going to watch it, I figured y'all should get to know him as well. So, the first fun fact about Josh is that he's never played a mobile before, which is the type of game League of Legends is. He prefers sandbox and dungeon crawl games. So, here's how this and most mobs work. There are two teams, three lanes in a jungle. Five players on each team, and whoever can kill the enemy's turrets and get to their nexus first wins the game. In fact, after 15 minutes, either team can just surrender if they want to end the game early. Now, this game like cake has layers, and the win condition I just explained is the first layer of understanding. Then, in order, the next things you need to understand is what the different lanes are, how the different champions work, how stats work, and then lastly, how items work. So, after I explain all those things, you'll be fully competent and know broadly everything about this game. And hopefully, you'll know everything about Josh, too, because another fun fact about him is sometimes he forgets to lock his window because he is a silly goose. Now, on to the lanes. If you grew up watching Khan Academy, this next part is going to be really fun for you. Each lane has a wave of minions, also known as creep, that spawns every 30 seconds. Killing these damn, this is hard to write. Killing these minions gives gold and XP. You use this gold to buy items for your specific champion, and you use the XP to level your champion and upgrade your stats and abilities. Now, in the top lane, you're going to Okay, this is this is too hard. I'm not doing this anymore. In the top lane, you're typically going to play a tank or a bruiser. Now, if you've ever slightly glanced in the general direction of a video game, you'll know what those terms mean. But if you're a young man going through your Rumspringer, then don't worry. I'll explain what that means in a sec. But top lane is typically very boring since tank champions don't do much damage. You're just there to farm minions for the first 20 minutes of the game. It's a low action lane that will allow you to practice CSing. CS stands for creep score, or how many minions you've killed. CSing just means killing minions, also known as farming. A lot of terminology in this game, but you'll get it. Mid lane is typically reserved for mages and assassins, so a lot of the burst damage on your team. Mid lane is pretty hard. You got six points of entry for enemies to come and gank you. Gank means invade a lane that isn't yours to help your ally kill their enemy laner. Top has one point of entry, making it the hardest to gank. And bot has three points of entry, so it's a nice middle ground. Not only that, you're the center of the map, so you're the closest to all the enemies. And you're typically playing a character that doesn't have a lot of health, so you're an easy target. There's a reason the best player in the entire world, Faker, is a mid laner. It's the hardest and therefore most impressive lane. Now, your bot lane actually has two players in it. Your attack damage carry and your support. You see, every champion has an auto attack where you right click an enemy and do some negligible amount of damage. But most champions rely on their abilities to do damage, not the attack damage carry, aka the ADC, though. The ADC builds items that specifically enhances their auto attacks, typically being the main source of consistent damage in long team fights. However, they rely strongly on items to carry them into the late game. So, they get a little butt buddy to keep them safe for the whole game. This is called your support. Now, unlike the other roles, the support doesn't have to worry about killing minions. They get a special item that gives them extra gold, and they let the ADC get all the minion kills. The support's job is to make sure the ADC has everything they need and want. This means three things. One, if you ever see anyone eating or having matching names, it's going to be the ADC in support. Two, Josh will probably want to play support because he's a very caring man based off of all the self-care books under his bed. And three, it is a thankless job. If you do good, everyone will congratulate the ADC, not realizing it was all you. It's not all bad, however. You get the widest range of characters as a support. You can play tanks, assassins, long range mages, arsonists, the list goes on. but you will never get the praise. Last but not least is the jungler role. This one is a bit odd because you don't belong to any lane. You see on Summoner's Rift, that's the name of this map. These are the lanes and everything else is the jungle. In the jungle, the mighty jungle, the monsters sleep tonight. There are designated spawn points for each monster. And it is the jungler's job and main source of income to kill these monsters and then gank. Remember that? We learned that a few lessons ago. There's going to be a test at the end of this, so be prepared. Then gank his allied lanes. Typically what the junglers are looking for in a gank is a lane that is pushed into the allied turret so the enemy has to run really far to get to safety so you have plenty of time to kill them. And that's it. That's how all the roles work. Now you'll come to learn that players don't always play the roles how they're supposed to. Like sometimes the ADC will play a mage or the jungler will be allergic to ganking just like how Josh is allergic to powderized chloroform. Another fun fact. Fortunately, you can experiment with what kind of champions you put where. But for that you got to know what they do. Every champion has four abilities that scale with different stats. But if I went to every ability, that would get complicated, and I'm going over stats later. So instead, here's every single champion in a nutshell. First, you have bruisers. Medium damage, medium defense. Typically melee with a nice all-around kit that gives decent defense and decent mobility. Marksmen, the showboers and entitled [ __ ] that expect everyone to work around them because they have horrible defense by themselves, but the most damage in the game. Assassin. In my opinion, the most fun class. High burst damage and high mobility. zero defense. They are made for getting in, killing one weak player, and getting out. They aren't made for long drawn out team fights. Tanks. They build a lot of health and resistances. So, if you're a big fan of resisting, like I know Josh is, you'll probably like a tank. They have high defense, high crowd control, like stuns and knockups, but low damage. And typically, they control the game. Team fights don't get won without tanks to soak up the damage. Next, we have mages. And this might be a good time to talk about different subasses inside of classes. You see, there are some burst mages, and then there are some battle mages. And every other class I just went over has different subasses. Now, I swear I'm not trying to confuse you, but all of those subasses can also just not be those classes. The character that you pick does matter, but you can also build certain items that change what class they are. For instance, this character is supposed to be built like a mage because that's what his abilities suggest, but I'm playing him like a marksman. Now, admittedly, a lot of people think this is really stupid, but I love it. And if I ever make another video, it's going to be about this. But when I say a burst mage, I mean a mage that can use their whole kit in 1 second and there goes all their damage. Whereas a battle mage is a mage that will stick around and keep casting abilities on account of low cooldowns. But the thing that makes a mage a mage is focusing on abilities that cost mana, building ability power, and typically staying at a range. And finally, supports. All of those types of characters can be supports if you're good enough and want them to be. And then there are some characters that just don't fit anywhere, like a character named Singed. He just runs around with a poison trail behind him. That's it. That's all he does. There's a lot of champions in this game and they're all pretty different. Takes time to learn, but if you want to know which one to play, just go to the collections tab, go to champions, click one you think looks cool, and click abilities and read what they do. And if you're in game and don't know the enemy champion, mouse over their name in the scoreboard, find their champion name, and look it up on the league wiki. It'll be a lot at first, but you'll get it down. Now, when you get in the game, you'll click this little button, and all these numbers will show up. These are your stats. Here's what they all do. Going left to right, starting from the top, we got attack damage. This is how much damage your auto attack does when you right click an enemy. Also, if you mouse over an ability, you can see what stat it builds with. For example, this ability does more damage when you build attack damage and AP. But even though it builds with AD, it only does magic damage, as it says right here, and it's in blue lettering. There's a bunch of different abilities in this game, and some of them scale with other stats like armor and health. So, if you want to learn your character, read the abilities and then read what they scale with, and then that'll tell you what items to build. Also, there's no benefit between magic damage and physical damage. The only difference is what they're negated by. There's a third type of damage called true damage, and it just ignores all damage reduction. It's super good, but very few characters have it. Next is ability power or AP. All it does is empower abilities that it scales with. So, most mages and supports build AP. All right, armor and magic resist. Building them reduces the damage you take from the respective type of damage. Armor reduces physical attack damage. Magic resist reduces magical damage. I'm hoping I didn't have to spell that out as clearly as I did, but I'm trying to make sure even if you are a complete idiot, this video will still cover everything you need to know. Armor and magic resist do have diminishing returns, though. You can't get to 100% reduced damage. For instance, if you have 100 armor, you can get 50% damage reduction. But if you have 300 armor, you get 75% damage reduction. Attack speed. Next, just how fast you attack. The number says how many attacks will go out in 1 second. It's capped at three. Ability haste reduces the cooldown or how often you can cast abilities. also has diminishing returns of the same value as armor and magic resist. Crit chance, it's just the chance that your auto attacks will crit and do a lot of extra damage. Move speed, it's just how fast you move. The number is how many units you move. It comes from allied champions, friendly minions, towers, wards, and even fae lights. Throughout Summoner's Rift, you're also going to find bushes of all shapes and sizes. Now, while they might look different, they all follow the same rules. And these rules are slightly different to your standard fog of war. Even if you have vision of the area around a bush, you still can't see inside that bush itself unless you have direct vision inside which you can get from any of the same sources. So, that's allied champions, minions, wards placed inside the bush, or even certain champion abilities. Anyway, wards. These are items you can purchase and even get given for free, that you can place on that map to give yourself and your team vision of the surrounding area. There are two types of wards to talk about here. You have stealth wards and control wards, which you'll often hear be called pink wards [music] for obvious reasons. Stealth wards, they're invisible wards that everyone starts with thanks to your free trinket, and that's this thing right here that we mentioned earlier, given to you automatically at the start of every game, which by default is going to be bound to four. These are free to use and they'll passively recharge over time. When you place them, they turn invisible after a short delay and they stay hidden from enemies unless they use special tools to detect those wards. As long as they're active, they're going to grant you and your team vision of the surrounding area. You can see how long your stealth ward will last by the white bar right here. Once these time out or get destroyed by your opponent, that ward will disappear and you're going to lose vision of that area. Control wards are a little bit different though, and they can be purchased for 75 gold in the shop. These not only provide you vision and last indefinitely, but more importantly, they reveal and disable enemy stealth wards in that area, allowing you to destroy them. With two limitations that we need to talk about here. They're visible to enemies, meaning if they spot them, they can see and destroy them, and you can only have one active on the map at a given time. This makes them super valuable for making sure certain areas like important objectives or gank paths stay completely under your team's control. Anytime during the game, you can also visit the shop and trade your yellow ward trinket for one of the two other options. The far sight or blue trinket, which basically just allows you to place a ward and gain vision from really far away, or the red trinket, known as Oracle Lens or Sweeper. This one plays an important role in vision mini-game, as when it's used, it's going to scan the area around your champion, revealing any hidden enemy stealth wards, temporarily disabling those and making them visible for just long enough for you to destroy them. Typically, they're picked up by junglers and supports. Now, you might remember earlier in the video when we mentioned that supports play a huge role when it comes to vision control. That's because as a support progresses their role quest, their starting item upgrades unlocking replenishable stealth wards that refresh every time they return to base. This makes supports the only role that can place wards consistently throughout the game outside of the single control ward you can have down and the basic free yellow trinket everyone gets. One brand new change to vision in 2026 though is the addition of Feylights, which you'll see placed around the map. What these do is they mark ideal spots to place your wards. Since not only are they positioned in high traffic areas and common ward spots, but when placing a ward in one, you'll also reveal an area around them, letting you spot enemies earlier and giving you more time to react. But now, let's jump to the good stuff. Choosing your champion. League, as we mentioned, has over 170 unique champions and finding one that fits your play style perfectly, it's going to take some time and that is okay. But in the meantime, we do have a couple of super easy to learn suggestions you should try out for each role. Starting off in the top lane, our first pick is Garen. Garen is the quintessential top lane bruiser and one of the most beginner-friendly champions in the entire game. His Q gives you a burst of movement speed, bonus damage on your next attack, and silences the target. His W grants bonus tank stats and when activated, gives you a shield that reduces incoming damage. And his E, well, makes you spin around and deal massive damage to anything around you. Then his ultimate is a crazy single-target execute that ignores defensive stats and it makes it really easy to finish off low-health enemies. Basically, his kit all fits together in the most intuitive way possible. You press Q to run at your target, you press W to take less damage, auto them once, and then hit E to spin to win, finishing them off with your ultimate once they drop low enough. And to top it all off, Garen's passive makes it really hard to deal with him in lane as you passively regenerate health when you haven't taken damage for a short time. What if bruisers aren't your thing? We get it and you think tanks might be more your play style, one of the best is undoubtedly Malphite. Malphite is, uh, well, literally an immovable object. Your Q is a ranged poke ability that deals damage, slows the target, and gives you movement speed. W gives you passive armor and on activation causes your next few attacks to do AoE damage. Then E is an AoE ground slam that does damage to nearby enemies and applies a debuff, reducing their attack speed by a massive amount. But what makes Malphite so dang strong is his ultimate, a dash that knocks up any enemies hit, dealing massive damage, making him one of the biggest team fight threats in the entire game. So, there's two champions in top. Moving over to the jungle, our first recommendation is Warwick. He's perfect for beginners as he's able to sniff out low-health enemies, giving you a visible trail right to them while you're clearing your camps. Q bites the target, dealing high damage and healing for yourself, while even letting you follow enemy mobility. W provides the blood trail and bonus attack speed against low-health targets and can even be activated to guide you to the closest enemy champion regardless of health. And E reduces damage and fears nearby enemies. And his ultimate is a long-range leap that locks down a target, deals big damage, and it also heals you. Oh, and his passive just gives Warwick extra healing from his attacks the lower he drops. All in all, this makes him one of the best duelist and 1v1 champions in the entire game. But if you're wanting to make more of an impression in team fights, then look no further than Amumu. He's tanky, he deals high damage, and he brings a ton of crowd control. Q pulls yourself to any target hit, it stuns them, and deals high damage. W passively deals damage to anybody near him, and E deals decent AoE damage and gets its cooldown reduced whenever Amumu gets hit. But what truly makes Amumu is his ultimate, a massive AoE effect that deals high damage and stuns any target hit. Single-handedly, this is going to allow you to carry games even as a complete beginner. All right, jungle out of the way, mid lane. If you enjoy mage-type champions, then our first suggestion is Arcane's very own from the hit TV series, Mel. Her passive has two parts. First, it makes last hitting minions an absolute breeze as you'll automatically execute low-health minions with your attacks and abilities. The second part causes your abilities to apply stacking marks to the target, which can be consumed using your ultimate for a big chunk of extra damage. Outside of that, Q fires a barrage of bolts in a small area, E is an orb that deals AoE damage, slows, and even roots targets, and her W is one of the most broken abilities in the freaking game as it allows you to reflect almost every spell in the game back at your opponent while also making you invulnerable. But if mages aren't your thing, I get it and you want to try an assassin, then look no further than Naafiri. Now, assassins are usually going to be weaker in lane due to their short range, but Naafiri's simple, powerful kit helps her bypass all that weakness that other assassins have. So, her Q throws daggers that deal high damage and apply a bleed. If you hit the same target twice, you're going to get bonus damage and healing. Her E is a short dash that deals high burst, and W gives her brief immunity along with bonus damage and movement speed. With her passive periodically spawning packmates that help with last hitting and significantly boost her damage. What really makes Naafiri shine though is her ultimate, a targeted leap that deals massive damage. So, after jumping in, you can chain your Q's and E to burst enemies down, then escape with W. And if you secure a kill or assist, you can even cast your ultimate again, leaping to the next target and gaining a shield. All right, moving on to the bot lane. Our first pick is Jinx, a champion that gives you the perfect feel for what playing ADC is all about. Her Q lets you switch between fast-attacking minigun and a long-range rocket launcher depending on what you need. Her W is a long-range skill shot that lets you poke or slow enemies, E places traps on the floor that root anybody who passes over them, and her ultimate is a map-wide rocket off low-health targets regardless of where you are. But what really makes Jinx exciting is her passive as whenever you get a takedown, you get a massive burst of attack and movement speed, letting you clean up fights. Great in team fights, amazing late game, and overall a solid ADC that is well worth putting the time into. But for a different take on ADC, you can instead try out Miss Fortune. Unlike Jinx and other marksmen, she is a bit more spell-focused in how she deals damage. And what that's going to do is it's going to quickly teach you how to balance dealing damage with your auto attacks as well as spells. Her passive makes her first attack on a target hit harder, helping with last hitting minions. Q is a shot that deals high damage and bouncing to targets behind. W gives passive movement speed lost when taking damage, but could be activated to gain the boost as well as some additional attack speed. And E is an AoE slow. But Miss Fortune is all about her ultimate, Bullet Time. Just look for a safe spot in a team fight, hit this, and watch as they completely melt. And last but not least, let's cover some support picks. If you're looking to heal, shield, buff, and keep your allies safe, then Milio does it all. Q deals damage and knocks targets away, W is an AoE field that also buffs allies inside it with bonus attack range, E shields allies and gives bonus movement speed with two charges, and your ultimate heals and removes debuffs from everybody near you, allowing you to get your whole team out of any crowd control. And on top of that, Milio's passive empowers allies hit by his abilities, causing their next attack to deal bonus damage. But if rather than keeping your team alive, you enjoy making plays, being a tanky frontliner, and locking enemies down, you need to try out Leona. Q is a targeted stun on a short cooldown, W provides bonus resistance for a short period, E is a root and a gap closer in one, and R is a long-range AoE stun. Her kit is just made to engage. You can E onto a target to root them, stun them with Q, then follow up with your ultimate, keeping the target locked down for a ridiculous amount of time. Now, when it comes to building your champion of choice, there's two things to consider, runes and items. However, unlike a lot of games, Riot has made this process a complete breeze for beginners. For runes, after selecting your champion, you can click this little button here and it's going to suggest three different rune pages with a short description of what they do. These are all, for the most part, just passives that either enhance your damage, survivability, or even utility in specific ways. A quick note here that if you're playing Swift Play, you're going to be able to select these alongside your champion before the game even starts. Then, the same goes for items in game. Once you open the shop, you're going to be able to view the recommended items along with a brief description of what they do. Now, while you can definitely make some slight optimizations to these as you learn more about runes and more about items, you honestly really can't go wrong here as all Riot does is pull the data for these items and rune builds from what other players are most commonly building on that specific champion. And one thing that might not be immediately obvious here to the layman is you don't need to save up for complete items. Just double click any complete item and the system will automatically buy available components based on your current goal. And you can see your progress toward that item in the bottom right right here below your inventory. Now, we touched on WASD earlier in the video, but even as a complete beginner, there's probably a ton of in-game settings that you might want to change right from day one. So, let's wrap this video up by going over a few of these. First, let's talk about your camera settings. By default, your camera is now set to dynamic, which automatically follows your champion and lets you look around slightly by moving your cursor to the edge of the screen. This is going to be great for complete beginners to MOBAs in general since it's going to help you stay centered around your character without really having to think or worry about camera control. That said, dynamic camera has some major limitations. It restricts how much you can easily see, which makes it harder to track enemies in fights and can really limit your awareness. Now, because of this, you'll never see any of the experienced players using this. Instead, the majority of players opt for free camera as this just gives you complete control. And you can then center the camera at any time by pressing or even holding space bar. Next, something we suggest that all new players do is to go to the interface and then reduce the size of the HUD. By default, this is set to 100, but we recommend somewhere around 50 as this just lets you see far more of your screen. Then, in the same section, we suggest increasing the size of your mini map. Now, like we mentioned earlier, your mini map is a crucial part of League and increasing the size can help with your awareness right from the get-go. Again, somewhere around the 50 mark is going to be great. And lastly, enable show spell cost, which is located right here. This simply just shows the exact mana cost for each and every one of your abilities on the icon, making it easy for you to quickly identify how much mana you need for each one as well as also tell you if you have enough for your full combo. And remember, everything that we've covered in this monstrosity of a guide is just the beginning. If you want to go deeper into the champions, deeper into the settings, and all the fundamentals that we talked about, check out our newly updated beginners course. This course is designed to put you right on track from day one, building the core skills and the habits every player needs from mastering champion abilities and combos to learning how to play each stage of the game with absolute confidence. We also dive much deeper into the settings, covering things like attack move for better kiting, quick cast for faster combos, and other must-have tools experienced players use every single game. And once you've built those fundamentals and you've mastered all the basics, the rest of our world-class courses are there to take you to the highest rank possible. So, if you want the best possible start to League of Legends and to improve faster than almost anybody else, be sure to check it out today using the exclusive beginner discount code in the description below. So, it has been a long ride, folks, and if you made it this far into the video, then thank you so much for watching. We put a ton of work into this. We wish you the best of luck, and we hope this video helped introduce you to one of the best games out there, man. We have a blast with it. Hopefully, you'll love it as much as we do. Catch you guys in the next one.

titre: Welcome to Noxus - Bite Marks (ft. TEYA) | 2025 Season 1 Cinematic - League of Legends
transcription:
C'est à Noxus que les forts
se rassemblent. Il recherche les braves
et les réfractaires. Il les appelle ici pour répondre à une simple question : Êtes-vous assez fort ? ♪ You say you’re not afraid to die ♪ ♪ But take off the armor ‘round your chest ♪ ♪ What's left inside? ♪ ♪ Lye-luh-lion licking your blade ♪ ♪ Do you really bleed if it washes away? ♪ ♪ Take a ride ♪ ♪ rough as you can ♪ ♪ Tell you a secret, right as your dogs are ♪ ♪ closing in ♪ ♪ I like it when ♪ ♪ the Bite Marks cut through the skin ♪ ♪ I like it ♪ ♪ when the Bite Marks cut through the skin ♪ ♪ Tell me the walls are closing in ♪ ♪ Into the fire and born again ♪ ♪ Taste the pain and drink it in ♪ ♪ I like it when the Bite Marks cut through the skin ♪ ♪ Lou-louder the bark and the bigger the blade ♪ ♪ One seat on a throne, one foot in the grave ♪ ♪ Lou-louder the moth then the bigger the flame ♪ ♪ Do you really bleed if it washes away? ♪ ♪ (Lou-louder the bark and the bigger the blade) ♪ ♪ (Lou-louder the bark and the bigger the blade) ♪ ♪ (One seat on a throne, one foot in the grave) ♪ ♪ (Don’t tell me you bleed if it washes away) ♪ ♪ I like it when the Bite Marks cut through the skin ♪ ♪ Tell me the walls are closing in ♪ ♪ Into the fire and born again ♪ ♪ Taste the pain and drink it in ♪ ♪ I like it when the Bite Marks ♪ ♪ I like it when the Bite Marks cut through the skin ♪ Chaque nouvelle voie semble nous rapprocher
un peu plus du chaos. Peut-être devrions-nous
nous concentrer sur l'opportunité qui s'est échouée sur nos rives. Les imprévus sont intrinsèques
à tout plan. Oui. Mais là, très chère,
cela va trop loin. Même pour nous. Piltover fut une leçon, Vladimir. La calamité est toujours à l'approche. À certains moments, son arrivée
est plus imminente qu'à d'autres. Notre prochain coup doit être
plus audacieux, pas plus prudent.

titre: What is League of Legends?
transcription: league of legends is a team-oriented strategy game the goal is to work together with your teammates to bring down the enemy nexus located in the middle of their base before your opponents take down yours that sounds straightforward but how you get there isn't so let's walk you through the basics you'll start by choosing one champion from a list of many all of whom have their own unique strengths and personalities some arranged marksmen pelting their opponents from a safe distance or burly fighters who like to get up in their opponents faces others are arcane mages casting powerful spells to trap and damage their enemies however you like to get things done there's a champion out there to match your style during the game champions get stronger in two ways the first of which is leveling up you gain experience by being in the area when enemies die and each level of experience gives you an ability point assign these points as you level up to unlock all of your abilities and make them stronger at level 6 you can unlock your ultimate ability which can change the tide of the game the second way to get stronger is buying items items are bought at the shop in your base using gold earned from killing enemies during a match once you've bought an item it goes straight into your inventory and you immediately gain its benefits and power up if you don't know which items to buy you can check the recommended tab in the store for some pretty good stuff for your champion once you know your way around the shop feel free to experiment with different item combos as you develop your own style but if you ever get lost the recommended tab has your back when you start the game you and your team will appear in the fountain this is also where you'll respawn if your champion dies this is a pretty safe place that restores your health and mana and provides access to the shop we just talked about you start off the game with some gold so grab some items right away and return to the shop when you've earned enough to buy more or to upgrade items you already own once you've got your starting items you'll head to one of the three lanes commonly called bot for bottom mid for middle and top for top between the lanes is the jungle a shadowy area filled with powerful monsters that can beat the heck out of an unprepared champion slaying these creatures will give you gold experience and some pretty sweet buffs but they do fight back so stay in the lanes until you feel strong enough to take them on each lane is a path between the two bases guarded by a set of powerful turrets you'll need to destroy your enemies turrets to get into their base and take down their nexus that's easier said than done though those turrets are perilous and even the most powerful champion will melt if targeted by one to safely take them down you need to stay behind your minions minions are computer-controlled units that march relentlessly down all lanes engaging any enemies in their path your opponents have minions too and you're gonna want to stomp them you'll get experience when they die but only the killing blow commonly called the last hit will earn you gold so time your attacks carefully once you and your minions push up to an enemy turret you'll have a few moments to damage it while it attacks your minions if you're in range of the turret without any minions around it will attack you so be aware of your position also if you attack an enemy champion while in range of the turret it will change its focus to attack you even if your minions are around working with your minions to siege enemy turrets is your main objective if you're looking to crack their base but watch out for enemy champions like you they have items and abilities and feelings to help them stop your advance and make their way to your nexus if you manage to take an enemy champion down however they are worth a nice chunk of gold sometimes things don't go as planned maybe an enemy champion gets the drop on you or maybe you take one too many turret shots whatever the case may be if your champion dies you'll have to wait for your depth timer to expire before respawning back in your bases fountain this timer increases as the game goes on dying early in a match only requires a few seconds of time out while a death later on could result in a 30 second penalty or even longer don't worry though you don't lose any items gold or experience on death and you can even buy items while waiting to respawn of course killing your enemies also takes them off the map for a brief period giving you the perfect opportunity to advance and demolish their turrets that's the basics of league of legends get money buy stuff and wreck their base all while battling against a team of opponents trying to do the exact same things to you a combination of skill speed and intelligence will lead you to the opponent's nexus and to victory then you can start a new game and do it all over again there's a lot more to the world of league of legends though a diverse community full of all kinds of people a competitive rank system to test your skills either solo or with a team of friends an ever expanding roster of champions to explore and master and even a professional esports scene where the best players in the world gather to play on a massive stage in front of thousands of fans it all starts with your first game so hit that big play button and start a game at first you'll have co-op vs ai available select intro bots for your first few games to help you learn the ropes try a few different champions each is different but there's nothing like finding a champion you really click with after you feel comfortable you can try other co-op difficulties or start playing against other human beings see you in the fields of justice and welcome to league of legends you

titre: Every League of Legends Rank Explained in 18 minutes
transcription: Today I'm explaining every rank in League of Legends. Iron. Iron is the very bottom of the ladder. The rank where dreams go to die before they're even born. It isn't even League of Legends. It's a psychological experiment. It's Riot's Daycare Center where every game is five people learning how to move their characters. You'll see things here that defy logic. This is where people purposefully build five tiers of the goddess on Garin. Not because they want to grief the game, but because tears look pretty. They then wonder why their spin doesn't hurt, but usually never come to the right conclusion. Games in iron are absolute chaos. Nobody farms, nobody wards, and objectives are just that thing in the river that kills you if you walk near it. What makes Iron unique is the unpredictability. Anything can happen. You might see a Blitzcrank support land every hook, but then forget to level up abilities, or a top lane Nus with 4,000 stacks who still loses because he only bought ability haste and nothing else. If you're here, don't worry. It doesn't mean you're hopeless. It just means you're at the tutorial stage. Champions that do well in iron are the ones that basically play themselves. Garen, Annie, Master Yi, and Malight. You press a button, something happens, and the fight is won. My advice for iron players is to not overthink it. Learn the basics. Try to last hit some minions, buy the recommended items, figure out what your champion does, find the recall button instead of endlessly dying to get back to the fountain. Learn that mini map isn't just a decoration. And for the last time, stop trying to solo Baron at level 12. Bronze. Bronze is the next step up. And it's where people think they're playing real League of Legends, but in reality, it's just a slightly upgraded Iron Circus. Here you'll see the classic all mid at 15 minutes strat because why farmside lanes when you can just bash each other's faces in mid lane? In reality, bronze is iron with a little more confidence, which is terrifying. Here, players know wards exist, and you pick one of the two approaches. Either you buy none of them because they deal no damage, or you buy two control wards on each recall and still miss three people who pass them on their way to gank you. Speaking of ganking, everyone's busy flaming the jungler for not sitting all day long in their 05 lane. All of this confidence results in every player thinking they're one clean play away from being Faker, but being unable to make one because they're held down by their teammates. The jungler thinks they're a carry. The AD thinks they're the main character. and supports. Well, supports still don't exist because everybody wants to be the main character. What makes bronze unique is that it's the birthplace of the ELO hell myth. In reality, bronze is where bad habits are born. There's constant fighting mid, spam fighting with no objectives, or running down bot lane because someone secured their kill. Champions that thrive here are stat checkers, Darius, Master Yi, Katarina. If your kit allows you to face roll the keyboard and still get kills, you're golden. Here's some advice. Stop fighting every second of the game. Learn how to push waves, when to recall, and I cannot stress this enough, play fewer champions. Don't lock in Yasuo because your team has four other knockups. Chances are they'll miss it anyways. Silver. Silver is the land of eternal suffering. People know the basics, but they're applied with the precision of a blinded Graves trying to hit his target. Everyone knows what should happen, but no one actually does it. Rotations. Half the team rotates for Baron while the other goes back to base to get a ruby crystal. Map awareness. Everyone gathers for a dragon fight, but it's still two minutes away from spawning. What makes silver unique is inconsistency. Some games look like gold. Some games look like iron. You'll see a jungler who perfectly times smite in one game, then forgets to buy the jungle item in another. Because people already understand the game a little bit, ego starts taking over in silver. That's their curse. Silver players often get stuck because they blame teammates for everything. I would have carried, but my top lane went 010. Buddy, you also went 010 two games ago. You just don't want to admit it. Silver is where games finally start to look like League of Legends. But like the knockoff mobile version. If they die to a gank, it's because their support didn't ping. If they lose lane, it's because top didn't teleport. But let's be real, Silver is mostly about players not watching the mini map and walking into obvious danger. Because there are some glimpses of teamwork in sight. Champions that do well in silver are reliable, simple, and consistent. Malight for the big R button or Ash for easy engages. Apart from that, there's also the double-edged sword named Teemo. Silver players have played League enough to harness deep hatred towards this champion. This is where his hidden global taunt passive comes from. Everyone wants to get this guy, and if they do, the Teemo player solo loses the game. If they don't, well then their mental explodes and Teemo's team gets a free win instead. My advice for silver players is to drop the ego and keep focusing on fundamentals. Last hitting, keeping an eye on the mini map, and controlling your tilt. If you can consistently farm 7 CS per minute, you'll leave silver behind faster than the pace at which the game is dying, according to some of League's content creators. And please, for the love of all that is holy, stop running it down because your jungler didn't gank in the first 5 minutes. Do that and you'll thank me later. Gold. From my experience climbing, this is the most relaxed rank in the entire game. Most people have already lost their ego in silver and have improved enough to understand that there's still a lot they have to learn. Gold is where people actually start to understand macro, dragon timings, basic warding, sweeping vision, and rotating to help side lanes. It's not perfect, but it's recognizable. League of Legends. This is the middle class of League with everyone in it thinking they're above average and hungry to prove it. What makes gold unique is the ambition. People start experimenting with builds, start testing pro strats, and some even correctly use teleport. You might even see some level one cheese invading enemy jungler in an attempt to win the game before minions spawned. And you better not go to the toilet at level one if there's a blitzcrank on the enemy team. Because of these good vibes, people tend to be quite optimistic, even if the game goes sideways early on. And you'd see some teammates trying their hardest to win even the hardest games while spamming no during every surrender vote. Champions that shine in gold are utility heavy champs that can swing fights. Jana, Oriana, Jarvin, even if you bring utility, you can still be useful despite mistakes. If you want to climb in gold, work on consistency. Try cutting your champ pool down to two to three solid picks for two rolls. Remain calm and collected and learn when to group. Gold isn't yellow hell anymore. It's the first real barrier for you to overcome. Remember, people can both carry you and allow themselves to be carried to a victory screen, but only as long as you stay positive without annoying everyone. Platinum. Platinum is the land of delusion. Everyone here thinks they're secretly challenger, but in reality, they're just the average platinum enjoyer. Even if wrong, this mindset is understandable. Plat mechanics start looking clean, combos look smooth, skill shots actually hit, and junglers know how to get to the crab before it even spawns. But it's also where ego peaks. If one thing goes wrong, you'll get a 40-minute TED talk about why your team sucks with an occasional slur included, and they'll probably be right about the mistakes they see. The problem is that everyone is blind to their own shortcomings. What makes Platt unique is the reliance on solo carry potential. Every player thinks they can 1v9, so team play goes out the window. Split push Fiora, check. 07 Talon still trying to assassinate the Fed AD, check. Champions that thrive are assassins and someone you can outplay others with. Draven, Leblanc, Kha'Zix. Platinum players have just enough mechanics to make these picks work, but not enough discipline to always win with them. The best advice for platinum players that I can give is to slow the game down and exploit all the opportunities. Let's say you got a lead in top lane. Instead of shoving each wave in and letting your opponent farm under turret, spend some time learning how to manipulate waves. Also, kills aren't the only way to get an advantage. Slow incremental wins add up over time and force opponents into making mistakes. And well, you're already good enough to punish them. While everyone's trying to do a quick one-two knockout, learn choking techniques instead. Oh, and mute chat. It'll save you years of therapy. Before we continue, please like this video. Thanks. Emerald. Emerald is the shiny new middle ground Riot added because too many Plat players were crying. This rank feels like a bridge between those that can carry with mechanics and those who actually understand macro. Games here look almost professional until someone from higher ranks comes over and exploits a billion small mistakes still present in each match. It's basically Platinum Plus, but with players who understand a little more about macro. Tilt is still overwhelmingly present. One bad fight and half the team AFKs mentally. Emerald players have solid mechanics, decent map awareness, and some draft understanding. This is where players start picking champions based on comps, not just comfort. And it is all visible in the champions that people pick up as direct counters. People might pick up champions with mechanically higher skill ceilings like Akali and Jace. Pair up Viego with an explosive team comp or install Silus the moment they see Malight on the enemy team. Emerald is also where support mains really start flexing their impact. Roaming Pike playmaking Rakhan or enchanters who actually properly time their shields. As there are many play styles in Emerald, the advice is more macro focused. Start tracking the enemy jungler and trying to figure out which of their camps are spawning soon. If you can guess where they are based on camps and timers, it's like playing with a free wall hack. Emerald is where knowledge starts outscaling pure mechanics. Diamond. Diamond is the rank where people start to stream. People here have refined mechanics, understand rotations, and actually punish mistakes. But it's also the birthplace of the toxic god gamer stereotype. If someone's bad, they'll tell them in chat, in pings, and probably in DMs afterward because they've seen some challenger streamer do that a day ago. The insults are usually quite creative in order to avoid automatic behavior systems. Not to mention, they'll come in their native languages, too. Diamond players know matchups, ward timings, jungle pathing, and how to set up objectives. They have actual game plans, even though their execution and teamwork still has loads to go. League actually looks like League and you'll stop seeing someone going 010 after directly countering their lane opponent. Champions that thrive here are skill expressive and high skill ceiling. Camille, Riven, Lein, Azir. At this level, raw mechanics and split-second decision-making decide games. Having this environment and actually being pretty good at the game means you can stream as you're better at the game than most of your viewers. Playing all the mechanical champs is bound to get you some highlights, which is perfect for your streaming career. So, here's my advice for diamond players. Focus on teamwork. Diamond is the rank where your ability to coordinate with randoms determines if you climb or get stuck forever. Learn to shot call, adapt, and stop insisting on playing your one trick if it ruins draft. Your individual skill is probably already good enough to hold your ground. But if it isn't, this is a time space chamber to hone your skills. Master. Master is where the grind truly begins. This isn't just ranked. This is a lifestyle. Master players are almost never someone who plays the game casually, queuing for two games and calling it a day. It's just not enough. So, while good casual players usually peak at diamond, these guys usually live and breathe league, watching League of Legends videos during increasingly longer Q times. Most masters have perfected their champs. No jungle tracking, no macro, and can punish any mistake instantly, usually destroying every rank below it without much difficulty. The game slows down here because every action is calculated, but the games can still go at rocket speed because of how good people are at snowballing. So, don't be surprised by the amount of games that end at 15 minutes with a unanimous surrender vote. Champions that thrive are impactful team fight and utility champs. Azir, Kai'Sa, Suani, Renekton. High impact picks that can swing team fights are very valuable. Besides that, you'll see many unique picks operated by OTPs who have perfected their favorite champion. Master ELO is where giving advice becomes increasingly difficult. Each case is very unique. Some players need to improve their mechanics, others their rotations, champion picks, item build paths, objective control, and so on. Every mistake is magnified, so consistency is the name of the game. Also, tilt management becomes huge, and the prevalence of challenger smurfs not caring about their rank anymore makes it even more important. It's not unusual to see people occasionally swing large 200 LP chunks in either direction, so the best climbs happen when negative loss streaks are eliminated from the equation. Grandmaster. Grandmaster is truly elite territory. Fewer than 1% of players ever reach this rank, and even fewer manage to hold on to it. Every game feels like a professional scrim, slow, calculated, and punishing. Mistakes are rare, and when they happen, they're punished instantly. Misplace one ward, waste one summoner, or take one bad fight, and the game can snowball out of control. At this level, everything matters. Drafting is critical. One bad pick can cost the entire match. Jungle tracking isn't optional. Players know timers, camps, and pathing like a second language. Rotations are sharp. No time is wasted. Every move is intentional. What makes GM unique is the mix of meta staples, and cheese counterpicks. You'll see standard power picks like Azir, Kai'Sa, or Seduani. But you'll also see surprise picks that punish predictability. Heimerdinger support, Shako jungle, or pocket picks players have mastered to perfection. These picks thrive because everyone respects meta champions, but not everyone is ready for a curveball. Chat usage and grandmaster is mixed. Some people write paragraph long plans designed to win the game. Some stick with just quick, short, one-worded commands. Some rely solely on pings. Everyone has their own idea of how to play the game. And the beauty is that it's refined so much. Everyone is mostly on the same page regardless. If the draft is clearly winning for one team, everyone knows that, which sparks action not usually seen in lower ranks. In other words, the lobby atmosphere is just as sweaty as the gameplay as some people will lock in Disco Noo-Noo in an attempt to force someone to dodge the game. Here's my advice for Grandmaster players. Treat every game like it's your career on the line. Dodge bad comps. Avoid games when your mental is off and never autopilot. Discipline is the only way to survive here because unlike lower ranks, you can't rely on your opponents to throw. If diamond is about proving skill and master is about consistency, Grandmaster is about precision under pressure. One slip and you're back down the ladder. Challenger. Challenger is the absolute peak of solo Q. The top few hundred players on the entire server. If Grandmaster is elite, Challenger is legendary. It's the rank everyone dreams of reaching. The rank that turns your name into a status symbol. The border shines. Your profile becomes intimidating. And every game feels like it's broadcast ready. You just know that you're making that screenshot the first time you reach this rank. Games here are faster, sharper, and more precise than anything below. One wrong ward placement can lose you Baron. One poorly timed recall can cost your team dragon. One single misstep in a lateg game team fight can decide the entire match. Challenger is not forgiving. It doesn't give you second chances. What sets Challenger apart is that every second is calculated. Rotations are instant. Vision control is airtight and players don't just play the map. They own it. Unlike Diamond and Below, you'll never see everyone randomly gather mid for a fight here. Instead, every push, every roam, every fight has a purpose. And who are the players at this rank? Everyone is either streaming their climb, grinding for pro trials, hoping to get scouted by an academy team, or already professional, sharpening mechanics before scrims. Champion pool and challenger is split between two extremes. First, there are top tier meta staples you are already familiar with. They are statistically efficient, always strong, and fit into multiple comps. Second, there are signature one-trick ponies that have mastered their picks to perfection. Thresh hooks never miss. Fiora split push entire games by themselves. veins dodge every skill shot. Rivens outplay 1v3 without breaking a sweat and so on. The truly unique thing here is that people start recognizing each other by name. This allows them to adjust their gameplay accordingly. If your lane opponent is Bows, you're buying Magis. If there's Malang on the enemy team, you're avoiding that level three gank like hell. If you've got Faker on the enemy team, you throw your hands up and pray for the best. So, what could my advice for challenger players be? At this level, mechanics don't matter as much. You're already at the top. Everyone has insane mechanics. Everyone can hit skill shots. Everyone knows matchups. The difference maker is discipline. Don't ego. Don't troll. Don't play ranked when you're tilted. Treat every single match like it's the last one keeping you in challenger. Here's the hidden truth about challenger. It's not just about skill anymore. It's about networking. Chatting with the right players after a match, knowing when to queue, dodging certain lobbies, and being part of the challenger circle all play a role in maintaining your spot. Sometimes surviving in challenger is as political as it is mechanical. If diamond is where you prove you're skilled, and master is where you prove you're consistent, challenger is where you prove you can perform under constant pressure. It's the top of the mountain, but the climb never ends because one bad streak and you're kicked back down. Thanks for watching. Please consider subscribing and see you in the next

transcription: 26.10 has some truly meta breaking champions as Smoulder is the most OP champ we've seen in years. Lichbane users are absolutely dominating the mid lane meta and Senna support is completely insane. So, we have all this and much more to break down in today's video as we provide you guys with an update to the tier list for every single role. Throughout the rift, a great migration occurs every 30 seconds. The minion wave, a vital food source for any climber who knows how to harvest it. But here we observe one of nature's most baffling behaviors. The hardstuck player abandoning his food source to chase a kill he will never get. Without proper wave management, he cannot survive. He cannot grow. He cannot climb. In the wild, this tragedy plays out thousands of times every minute. But today, something is different. Today, he discovered a powerful new tool. Skill Cap season 16 macro and wave control courses designed to teach even the most lost climber the secrets of the rift, walking you through every minute of the game step by step from the very first wave all the way to closing out games cleanly. And unlike other guides in the wild, we focus on teaching you the macro concepts that actually work in solo Q. and most importantly, strategies you can execute without having to rely on the rest of your pack, which paired with our newly updated essentials courses covers everything you need to master season 16 for every role, giving you the complete road map to climb this season. And best of all, you can do it all completely risk-f free as you're backed up by a rankup guarantee. If you somehow don't climb at least five divisions while actively using our service, then you get every penny back. For the first time in his life, he is no longer hard stuck. Check out the link in the description below for an exclusive onetime discount code. So, top lane is usually a role that has quite a few updates each and every patch, but 26.10 was an outlier. As a result, we will continue to have Teemo and Kale rated as the most OP top laners for solo Q, as they are incredibly powerful with the recently reworked static Shiv. Shiv, Teemo players are winning a whole 3% more than Nasher's players. So, it's pretty crazy how one item change can completely shift the power of a champion. Wukong, Umbessa, and Anivvia were the only relevant top laners that were changed for this patch. And here's how things have landed. Anivvia actually did end up losing quite a bit more power than we were initially expecting. Base stat changes never seem that crazy on paper, but always end up hurting quite a bit as Anivia lost 2% win rate from her armor nerfs. As a result, we have dropped Anivvia down into the top lane Btier. Woo and Ambessa changes more so aligned with expectations as Wukong is a really good and underrated A tier pick right now. While Ambessa is slotting in as an average Btier champ. So, moving on to jungle. Now, we noted in the pre- patch tier list how if the Lichbane changes landed impactful, then Niti would be in need of a push up the tier list, and it's played out in that manner. Lichbane buffs have been huge for a wide variety of champs, and especially for Niti because she's a champion who loves rushing the item. Movement speed as a stat is incredibly valuable for solo Q and even more so on Niti. Even if you're just one or two seconds quicker to a fight, that can make or break whether you win it with Niti. Because if your teammates go in and land their CC and you're just barely in range to hit your spear, then that 2% boost in movement speed from Lichbane can be the difference between you landing a spear or not. Niti has seen a bit over a 1% increase in her overall win rate from the Lichbane buffs. So nothing over the top that makes her OP, but enough to where she's being moved out of the jungle B tier and into A. So even though Riot had the lease in changes under the adjustments category of the patch notes which tends to mean that they will land more power neutral, Lee has most definitely been buffed by the changes. We note in the pre-patch tier list that we were leaning more towards Lee gaining power because the W change over a large sample size is just such a nice buff. The fact W is now on a much shorter cooldown if you use it to minion or ward and you also gain the shield means the solo playmaking potential of Le has gone up a bunch and that's what you want in solo Q. Even though W is slightly nerfed when used to an ally, the trade-off for having a stronger W when used to wards is well worth it. And then of course, Lee's R got a really nice quality of life change that definitely won't impact you in every single game, but will feel nice to hit from time to time where when you land the killing blow with R, their body will still fly through the air, enabling you to get the knockup. So, because these changes have landed quite positively for Leon, he's going to be promoted up into the jungle S tier for 26.10. One of the biggest winners from the jungle in 26.10, 10 who has seen a significant spike in power due to his buffs is Wukong. Wukong had been completely forgotten prior to 26.10 with a play rate sitting below 2% but things have really flipped due to his buffs. Play rate has more than doubled to over 4% while win rate has shot up by about 2% as well. So the changes have turned out to be extremely impactful. So for the first time this season, Wukong is being moved up into the jungle S tier as one of the better solo Q champs. So for the jungle tier list, we have a couple of minor adjustments for mid patch as Nocturn is going to stand alone as the most OP jungler for solo Q. Shyvana nerf did end up hurting her a bit, so we decided to drop her out of the OP tier and into S. However, that doesn't really mean she's much weaker at all. Still a great pick, but just not on the same level as Noctturn. And then same for Warwick, we are moving him into S as Knock is just the clear-cut overperformer in this current jungle meta. Similar to Niti, Evelyn is a jungler who gained a bunch of power for this patch due to the Lichbane buffs, and she's slotting in as one of the best S tier picks of the patch. Udier is in such a strong yet underrated state right now for solo Q because both AD and AP builds are equally as powerful after the AD buffs he got last patch. You can play the Spear of Shojan rush setup with a Q max if you need more physical damage or the Leandre's rush with R max if you want magic damage. So it gives you a ton of versatility and makes Udier one of the best blind pick junglers in the game. In regards to the Quinn jungle buffs that Riot issued, it looks like they haven't made her a viable jungler. Based on the data that's come through so far, Quinn Jungle is hovering around a 42% win rate, which means she doesn't even qualify to be placed on the tier list at all. We'll have to see if things progress at all over the next patch as players look to optimize Quinn Jungle, but as of right now, it's not looking very promising. And then for an update on Hecrim, he is now back to taking Storm Raiders due to the buffs it received this patch. In 26.9, Conqueror was outperforming Storm Raiders, but that is now flipped for 26.10 with Storm Raiders Hecrim players winning more than Conqueror. So, we've seen Shinszou pulled out a bit in the top lane after his recent 26.9 changes, but he's actually performing even better as a mid laner in 26.10. Mid lane, Shinszou has actually started to see a decent amount of play, holding a play rate up around 1.5%. Shin as a mid laner makes tons of sense when you consider he's got the range poke tool from W. And once you complete your static shiv, things become extra silly. Our challenger midlus discovered that if you proc the shiv energized effect on the enemy, then it's going to extend the range of Shinszou's E. You don't even need to land your W to get the increased range on E. If shift proc lands, then you have the instant insane engage range. This just means that when playing into squishy mid lane champs and on shiv completion, your ability to dash onto them and chunk them out is super easy to do. And a lot of players have never played against Shinzo mid or even know of this interaction with Shiv. So, you'll be catching them off guard and securing so many free kills. In his limited amount of play, the only matchup that is giving Shin a ton of issues right now is Vex. This makes sense because Vex has her fear, which is very easy to proc. So it makes it harder for Shin to dive in and be as aggressive as he wants. For the core build on your Shinzou mid lane, it's a Shiviver Rush into Dusk and Dawn second, while Nashers or Rift Maker are viable third options depending on if you want more split push threat from Nashers or better tankiness and team fight impact from Riftmaker. And then for the keystone rune, you're taking Hail of Blades so that your bursts and ability to proc your knockup from Q is extremely reliable. If you're in a matchup where the enemy has more mobility though, say Leblanc or an Ahri, consider going Storm Raiders Surge over Hail of Blades. Storm Raiders is performing quite well after the buffs it got this patch and will give you a lot of additional sticking power into higher mobility champs. So for Shinszou's tier list placement, he's going to be placed in the mid lane S tier as one of the best champs you can play for the role in 26.10. So as noted during the intro, mid laners who like to run Lichbane have all seen a nice surge in power for 26.10. We're talking Fizz, Katarina, Diana, Ekko, and Twisted Fate. Each one of them are significantly better, and every single one of them is placed in the S tier or higher. We noted in the pre-patch tier list that Ekko was one to keep an eye on specifically because he still has the option to run Dusk and Dawn. From the data that's rolled in so far, Lichbane and Dusk and Dawn are actually performing equally as well on Ekko so far. This is exactly what you want to see because now it opens up variety and gives Ekko the ability to run one or the other based on what he thinks will suit him best for the enemy team comp. Lichbane offers up more burst. So, in games where the enemy team is majority squishy and you can just play for one shots, then going Lichbane makes sense. The enemy team is more tanky though and you'll need to take extended fights to win out. That's where Dusk and Dawn value shoots up since you're more durable and have more ability haste. So overall, don't sleep on Lichbay and Ekko. Now for 26.10 as it is back to being a viable alternative to Dusk and Dawn. So breaking down the mid-lane tier list for 26.10. The OP tier is going to be locked down by Zerith and Fizz. We were correct in our assumption going to the patch that Fizz would emerge as one of the best solo Q mids due to the Lichbane buffs. Katarina and Ekko are both being promoted up into the S tier as well because they are finding a ton more success with those Lichbane buffs too. In the past few patches, Katarina has had a couple of viable rush items in Gunblade, Lichbane, and Kraken. But with these Lichbane buffs going through, it's just overperforming miles ahead of Gunblade and Kraken. Now, Zed nerfs did turn out to be a little more impactful than we had hoped. So, he will be dropping down one more tier and into A for 26.10. Same thing with Anivvia. The nerf did land slightly more significant than expected. So, she will be dropping down from S into A. Oriana is going to be moved out of the C tier as the buffs to Storm Raider Surge have helped her out a decent amount. Still not a top tier mid laner, but no longer in the gutter. So for the ADC tier list in 26.10, nothing actually changed in regards to the top picks in meta. And Smolder actually gained in win rate despite already being an OP tier pick in 26.9. This forced Riot's hand to issue more Smolder Hotfix nerfs. And here's what they ship. Base AD went down from 60 to 58. Q damage was lowered by 20 at max rank. Crit scaling has been increased from 50 to 75%. And then our damage has gone down with the base dropping by 50 and the bonus AD ratio lowered by 10%. So from what we're seeing so far, these hot fix nerfs have been somewhat significant, but not enough to warrant dropping Smolder out of the OP tier. Keep in mind, Smolder had a 55% win rate to start the patch. So even with these nerfs dropping that by 2 or 3%, he still outperforms every other ADC in the game. It's not till 26.11 to where Riot will be nerfing the interaction between Black Cleaver and Deathfire Touch, which is the big change that could actually push Smolder out of meta. It's kind of funny because with Smolder having a 25% play rate and a win rate at 54%, you'll notice that the win rate of most ADCs has gone down for this patch, with Smolder showing up one in every four games and winning at the rate he has been. It's led to almost every single other ADC losing around 0.5 to 1% win rate. So, most ADCs are actually sitting at win rates below 50% right now, which is something we very rarely see. If Smolder is banned out and you want another really good champ to abuse for this patch, while the DFT and Black Cleaver interaction still exist, then consider picking up Senna. Senna is a top tier pick for both ADC and support right now as she runs the core of Shiv with Black Cleaver paired with Deathfire Touch which has immense synergy. One ADC who got buffed this patch was Zary and it's turned out that the changes weren't actually that impactful at all. On paper they look pretty significant but based on the data they haven't moved the needle with Zer gaining less than 0.5% win rate. So we're moving her back down into the B tier and this is even with the bait item in static Shiv being built less for this patch. So even with the proper itemization, Zary is barely performing better than before these buffs. So, for the support tier list in 26.10, the only change we'll be making is promoting Senna up into the OP tier to join Saraphene. Senna is incredibly powerful right now with her black cleaver rush and deathfire touch combo. Whenever you quue a target, you will gain five stacks of the cleaver passive almost instantly, so it just makes cleaver insanely reliable on the champion. Black cleaver was always a good purchase on Senna, but it's become OP due to its death fire interaction. According to Riot, they will be nerfing this interaction for 26.11, so take advantage of the build while you can. for a full extensive breakdown of the setup. We've got it all laid out nicely up on our new free build site for you guys. So, overall, support did not see a single highly picked meta support changed in 26.10, which means the tier list is looking nearly identical to 26.9. Deathfire Touch nerf has negatively impacted Brand a little bit, but we already moved him into the A tier during the pre- patch tier list, and that's where he will stay as a result of the nerf. But while lists like these show you what to play, our brand new season 16 essentials courses for every role teach you how to win. Each designed to walk you through every stage of the game from start to finish. From the exact trading and recall timers you need to win lane to the mid-game macro decisions 99% of players get completely wrong, all the way to the team fighting tips and tricks challengers abuse to always deal the highest damage possible. Whether you're a top laner in the most unfavorable matchup possible, a midlaner having to deal with everybody in your lane 24/7, a jungler trying to balance ganking with farming, an ADC stuck trying to hard carry with a support who just exists to grief you, or a support that's struggling to carry games on your own. Our essentials courses are tailor made to focus on the exact concepts that actually matter for climbing on your role in your ELO. Check them out today completely risk- free using the link in the pinned comment or description below. As if you don't climb at least five divisions, you get every penny back. So, there you have it everyone. That's a complete look at the solo Q tier list for every role as we reach the midway point of 26.10. Thank you so much for watching and we'll see you in the next one.
