titre: 15 SECRET Jungle MECHANICS You NEED to Know! - League of Legends
transcription: Did you know you can do both red and Raptors at the same time? Or that you can also do Krugs [music] and red together, too? Or how about blue and Gromp? Riot supposedly removed the ability to double camp a few years ago, but still, there's tons of hidden jungle mechanics that 99% of players just don't even know about. Some are simple and some are practical things that you should be abusing every single game, while others are crazy tips and tricks [music] that once mastered can give you a huge advantage. As today, we're going to be breaking down 15 hidden mechanics, jungle routes, and so much more. So, let's jump straight into it. There is a secret mechanic that constantly trolls your clear every single game and nobody knows why. Everybody already knows how leashing and patience works. When engaged, the large monster in a camp has a [music] patience bar above their health bar. Additionally, there's also the circle surrounding the camp as well. If the camp leaves this circle, it'll cause the patience bar to tick down. That is, unless the camp's outside, but you're still inside. Either way, once you both leave that circle, this patience [music] starts ticking down and the camp will reset after exactly 4 seconds. Which of course, as we know, top junglers always look to take advantage of to speed up their clear. But the big issue that nearly every jungler runs into is that there's another hidden mechanic at work here. As that patience bar is actually only for the large monster in [music] that camp. Once it dies, the smaller monsters have their own patience circle, which is not only completely invisible, but it's a whole lot smaller. You can see this for yourself by pulling a multi-target camp to its max range, then killing that large monster. The little monsters just end up resetting after a few seconds, and this is why you're going to often have those super awkward moments when you have to run reluctantly back to kill one of the small Krugs or Raptors [music] when trying to min-max your clear. And as we all know, this mostly happens on Krugs, but there's a few solutions to this. First, always save your AoE ability for when the medium and large Krugs are about to die. Like here, although Lee could finish off the big ones faster by using his E, he saves time overall by holding it until they both die so he can instantly one-shot all the small ones. Now, if you don't have an AoE ability ready though, don't worry. Avoid kiting past the patient's range and then once the [music] Krugs have spawned, land two autos first before you start kiting and then kite away. This is going to be exactly enough pet damage combined with one more auto as you kite to finish them all off. And lastly, you're able to leave either one mini Krug, one small wolf, or one small raptor alive and still hit level three from your first quadrant. And if you're doing Krugs last, you can leave up to four alive and still hit level four if you need to get to somewhere fast. But min-maxing patience as a whole is undoubtedly the most important mechanic that every jungler really has to master. So, take a simple champion like Nocturne. If you just stand and kill each of your camps barely kiting or maximizing patience, you're going to finish your clear at a little over three minutes. But with just some pretty basic kiting when mobs are low, you can get this down by around 10 seconds. However, mastering your kiting and taking full advantage of patience, pulling camps to you, and even double camping where possible can get that down to as low as 2:42. That's over 15 seconds that you can save on just your first clear. But after four clears, you're going to have had a whole extra minute of time to gank, invade, and take objectives, whatever you want to do. And not to mention, 15 seconds is the difference between getting to scuttle right as it spawns or being able to gank before it, potentially picking up a first blood that completely snowballs the entire game out of control, which is why that we make sure to have the world record clears for every meta champion in the entire game inside of our clear course, each with super helpful subtitles to guide you through your clear step-by-step, including the exact health thresholds to start fully maximizing patience, champion-specific tips, and champion-specific tricks that only OTPs know about, and so much more. And after you've mastered your clear, you can take our brand new Jungle Essentials course. With countless five-star reviews, each chapter is focused on teaching you the practical advice that actually helps you climb in your [music] Elo, such as how to balance your time between ganking and farming, what to do when all your lanes are inting, and secrets that you need to know about objectives and priority. Check it all out today completely risk-free using the link in the pinned comment or description below. As if you don't climb at least five divisions while [music] actively using our service, you get every penny back, no questions asked. But anyway, let's get back to the video. Now, back to that patience range that we just talked about. A few years ago, Riot made these ranges drastically smaller across the entire jungle. And why did they do this, you might ask? Well, they were trying to kill a mechanic that's called double camping, where, you guessed it, you'd pull two camps together and kill them both at the same exact time. For [music] AoE junglers, let's just say this was absolutely absurd. You could cycle your entire jungle so insanely quick that it just wasn't even fair. And so, Riot, they nerfed the ranges on this. And double camping from that point was a thing of the past. Or, so they thought. Because players are still finding hidden ways to do it despite the shorter patience radius. And so, the most practical use of this is for doing Gromp and blue at the same time, which, honestly, for red side, couldn't be easier to replicate. Just start your blue as normal, but pay attention to these two little rocks right here. For melee champions, you can click on the rock on the right, smite your Gromp to pull it to you, and then click on the rock on the left to be in position. This way, you're going to be perfectly in the center of both camps' patience range. And as long as you don't move, neither of them will reset. Meaning, if you have AoE in your kit, like Hecarim does here, you can just spam damage and kill both of them at the same time. But even for champions without a spammable AoE, this is still considerably faster thanks to the additional damage of your jungle pet. So, for ranged champions, you can do the exact same thing, but instead pull Gromp to you with an ability. And this is obviously what's completely insane on Fiddlesticks. But for any champion, it's honestly far easier to replicate than it sounds, and you should be able to get it down in just one or two tries. And this right here is why League just is not a balanced game. As for red side, although this is technically still possible, it's just way, way too inconsistent to pull off. But one of the craziest double camps that nobody knows about is doing red and raptors together. There's actually a few ways that you can do this, too. If you have the ability to pull a camp from range, you can place a ward on your red, pull the small raptor from max range, and then you'll quickly smite your red to pull it towards you, and then see this little flower here? Instantly click slightly under it into the left of it, and you should be in position. Now, just to be clear, this is not easy to pull off, but if you're able to master it, the payoff can be absolutely insane, especially on champions with a lot of AoE damage at level one. But ranged champions with dashes like Graves and Kindred can also look to abuse this as well. Just start on your red, pull it, then dash over this wall here, and instantly hit the closest raptor before heading to the same spot. Definitely a cool little mechanic to min-max here. And that's not all. You can even do Krugs and red at the same time. And although it's really only Sett and Mordekaiser who can do this reliably, and ideally you're going to be needing to start on your blue side jungle and clear to red. Then, when you do get to your red buff, simply pull it, walk down to the Krug wall here, and use your E to pull the big Krug towards you. It's going to clip through that wall, and you can auto both the entire time. Kayn is also another champion who can do this, although it is a whole lot harder to do with him, as you're going to need to clip through the wall yourself, but if done correctly, you can just AoE down both camps. Definitely something worth practicing if you're a Kayn main. Now, there's a ton of hidden mechanics in the jungle, and a large amount of them revolve around this little guy here. How your jungle pet works is that after you deal damage to a camp, your pet will then deal AoE damage to that entire camp afterwards two times. This is to help junglers without AoE damage clear camps like Krugs and raptors. And for the majority of your clear, you can just focus the highest health monster and just let your pet take care of the smaller ones. Now, there is a trick to abusing this though that you can speed up your clears by just a few seconds with and it's super simple to understand. Basically, whenever you're looking to kite to camp towards the next one or even out of its patience, if you just move between each of your autos, you're barely going to cover any ground. But, let's say your wolves are low and you want to start kiting towards Raptors. Then, instead, you should actually space out your auto attacks. So, you auto once, let your pet hit twice, then auto again. This lets you cover a lot of distance and it will still maximize your damage. And speaking of maximizing your damage, did you know the runes you pick at champ select can actually change how hard your pet hits? This is because your pet's damage scales from your max health. This means taking the extra early HP from flat health instead of scaling can surprisingly shave off a few seconds on your early clears. And that's minor, but again, every second can really end up counting, especially in more difficult early game matchups. But, jungle pets also come with some major down sides that you absolutely have to know about. First, you get massively reduced experience from all lane minions early on. This starts with you getting only 30% of the experience for the first 2 minutes and then it slowly ramps up with you getting 37% at 2 minutes, 44% at 4 minutes, 65% at 10 minutes, and then 93% experience at 18 minutes with the debuff being fully removed at 20 minutes. Basically, this means sharing experience with your allies is extremely bad early into the game. As not only are you griefing them, but you're also doing it for very little gain for yourself. Now, the only exception to this reduced experience is if you're 1.5 levels behind the average game level, then you don't get this penalty. Now, that said, you should still obviously look to pick up any free waves going to waste in the early game as you still get full gold. That is, until your total gold earned from minion kills is greater than 40% of your gold from jungle camps. As then, killing a minion grants you 13 less gold and 50% reduced experience. Now, this should rarely happen unless your laners are of course just permanently dying and you're constantly soaking up waves. But, you're going to know if you have this debuff as it shows this icon here above your skill bar. Basically, if you have it, just ignore lane minions altogether entirely as they're going to be completely worthless. And on the topic of experience, the [music] most important jungle camp on Summoner's Rift right now is the second scuttle crab. And if you've watched pro play anytime recently, you are already going to know why. After jungler's full clear two times and get a scuttle each, which is pretty standard for most games, there's only one camp left, the second scuttle crab of the game. And crucially, it gives you exactly enough experience to hit level six and unlock your ultimate, which can be a huge spike for the team with the jungler that secures it. And as this happens just after the 5-minute mark, means it can often secure you the first dragon of the game or ensure you win any skirmish that happens. But, that's not the only way to secure the first dragon because top players have found an insanely smart mechanic that can make the first dragon or basically guaranteed. And the trick is all to do with jungle treats. Everyone knows that if you kill a camp, you feed your pet one treat. And at 15 treats, your pet evolves and you get your first smite upgrade, taking the damage from 600 to 1,000. Only if you were to start on the top side and clear down after that second clear, you'd actually be missing one treat for the smite upgrade. But, there's a mechanic put in the game to help you catch up if you lose camps or haven't been farming well called bonus treats. That when consumed, provide you an extra treat per camp. And you can absolutely abuse this mechanic to always reach dragon with your smite upgraded. All you have to do is base after wolves or raptors on your second clear, in turn, delaying either your gromp or krugs just a little bit. At which point, once it hits 5:30, you gain a bonus treat. Meaning, by killing that last camp on your second full clear, you get your smite upgrade. So, you can either start dragon yourself and do it considerably faster with the extra damage from your smite, assuming you have the opposite side to the enemy jungler, or just guarantee you win any smite fight if you happen to path the same way. Oh, and a random quick side tip here that we still see many players fail to use is that once your smite fully upgrades and you consume all treats, it makes it AoE. So, always make sure to pull multiple camps together to get full advantage of this later in the game. But, moving away from mechanics for just a moment here, it's time to talk about having. As there's a new jungle route players are starting to take advantage of to completely counter one of the most common strategies junglers have been doing for years, the raptor start. As you more than likely know by now, it's super common for farm-heavy junglers to start on their raptors. The reason for this is that it gets your smaller camps respawning [music] faster, so you can cycle your jungle a little bit sooner and more frequently throughout the game, making it common on champions that either want to power farm or hit level six quickly. But, high elo players know that if you start raptors and full clear, you need to backtrack immediately to your raptors after you finish your first scuttle. Otherwise, if you wait anytime looking for ganks or even basing for an item, the enemy jungler, if they path the opposite way to you, can just do their scuttle and steal your raptors right after for nothing. And there is a way to completely abuse this. What you're going to need to do is at level one, go get a ward on the enemy raptors just to confirm that they're starting there. As we mentioned, this is going to often be farm-heavy junglers that are trying to get to level six fast like Nocturne, Amumu, and even Shyvana, or even just the ones with really good AoE at level one like Lillia and Kayn. Once you get confirmation that they're starting there, you want to make sure you start on your buff opposite and path the exact same way as them. So, like this Rengar here, he starts his blue to path to top exactly like Karthus. Then, once he finishes his Krugs after a full clear, here's the secret tech. Rather than going to the same scuttle as your opponent, instantly recall, spend your gold, and head to the opposite scuttle. This way you can clear it and then go straight into their [music] raptors. Why this is so broken is that there's two outcomes to this. You either get raptors for free, meaning you're up one camp and then can go back to your own jungle if they waste any time after their scuttle, or if they try to contest, you now have an item advantage over them. Meaning, [music] if they try to fight or a 2v2 breaks out around mid lane, you have a massive advantage that practically insures you come out ahead. And quickly, while we're on the topic of invades here, a little hidden piece of tech is that you can completely abuse vision mechanics to steal away buffs from your opponent. Every game behind your blue buff, there's going to be a blast cone that spawns. You can angle this to go directly into the bush near your opponent's blue, and surprisingly, they don't see you while you're in the air. If you know your opponents are starting on their blue, you can sneak into this bush right here, wait for the blast cone, then go right over. Sit in that bush, and then go for the easy unexpected smite steal. Which means, you're now going to be level two and full health, and your opponent has no choice but to just concede the rest of their jungle, or just die if they don't flash away. But anyway, back to the roots as there's yet another new one challenger players have been abusing that prior to this season was only ever possible on just a few champions. And how it works is simple. You start Raptors, then do red with smite, and then after that do Krugs. But from here, you immediately press base, and as long as you didn't waste gold on a health potion, you're going to have around 425 gold. Meaning, you can buy a long sword, dark seal, or even an amp tome, plus even grab a pink ward if you got one of the former. From here, run straight to your wolves, and just as long as your champion can go over this wall here, then you can do wolves, Gromp, and blue as normal. And as you have that extra bit of AD or AP, you're going to finish your clear around the same time you usually would with a standard full clear, getting to your scuttle just as it spawns. Only now, if your opponent doesn't know about this tech, then you have an item advantage, practically guaranteeing you win any potential scuttle fights. And this is something almost every champion can reliably pull off if you get faster than you improve at your first clear. But if you really want to take your jungle to the next level this season, then we just dropped a brand new five-part jungle essential scores for season 16 that covers everything you could ever need, including the exact rules challenger junglers use to never waste a single second on the map, when to farm, when to skip camps, and how to turn your dead time into free kills and objectives that the enemy jungler just cannot answer. We even reveal the pathing tricks and the counter jungling strategies high elo players are abusing this season to completely one v nine, as well as everything that you would ever need to know about ganking and exactly how to navigate macro and objectives in the mid to late game. So, you are never going to get flamed for being in the wrong place at the wrong time again. And look, you pair this up with our world-famous jungle clear course with the fastest possible clears for every meta champion, and you've got everything you could ever need to climb as a jungler this season. And we are so confident this works, it's all backed up by our rank up guarantee. Climb at least an entire division while actively using our service, or you're going to get every penny back. Gain access to all of this and more today using the special one-time discount code in the description or pin comment below, and finally hit the rank that you deserve this season. But for now, thank you all for watching, and we'll catch you in the next one.

titre: I Spent a Week Playing League of Legends Ranked as a Beginner
transcription: This is League of Legends, one of the most popular games in the world. Known for its difficulty, strategic death, and it's very, very intriguing community. I decided to give myself one week to hit bronze. A simple task, right? Well, you would think so. I've reached top 500 in other games, so I also assumed it would be pretty easy. Now, all I had to do was hit level 30. Honestly, it took me so long, I almost gave up. Fortunately, I did end up making it. My journey to become a legend can now begin. But first, I'll explain the basics of the game so we're all on the same page. There are five positions. Each team has a top lane, jungle, mid lane, ADC, or support. The goal of the game is simple. Destroy the nexus of the opposite team. To do so, you must break the turrets in order to gain access to the inhibitors. By breaking these, you gain access to the nexus. There are also objectives around the map, such as dragons and barren, but you'll get to know them throughout our journey. As for the characters, there are 170 of them. I decided I wanted to play Ahri at first and picked up Mel as a secondary in case Ahri got banned. and I had only practiced mid lane until now. Keep that in mind. I then set a couple goals I wanted to achieve aside from just hitting bronze to help me measure my improvement. I wanted to have one game with zero deaths, a game where I got 10 or more kills, and lastly, achieving rank S+, which is a rank that's given to you after every game to give you an idea of how you perform, S+ being the highest. After that, I launched my first rank game ever. This would be my first of five placement games. Unfortunately, I was immediately hit with a reality check. What? How is this possible? Although I didn't manage to trade my position for mid, it was definitely a scary sight. It wouldn't matter as someone would end up dodging the game, so I had to pray I would get my preferred role. I can't get my role. Yeah, it was definitely not the start I was looking forward to. I lock in Jinx since she's probably the only ADC I ever played and start praying that my team can carry me. Start of the game wasn't as bad as I was expecting. We get jumped early on, but I'm able to trade myself and my team finds another kill shortly after, allowing us to go 2-1 during that fight. I found myself getting poked constantly and was struggling a lot since I didn't know any of the champions abilities. I then proceed to run into their retired team while they're doing the dragon. Thankfully, I'm able to run away and back to my base. I come back and grab a quick kill onto Caitlyn, allowing us to grab the first tower bot. We run it down mid to grab a quick tower along with the kill. At this point, the game was basically over and the enemy team understood that. First game, first win with a surrender. Everything I needed to boost my confidence. We then got to see what rank I would start at. Not what I was hoping for, but it shouldn't make things too hard either. titre: I always hated Yone for how easy his damage scales. Let me show you why.
transcription: Hey everybody, welcome to the next commentary. Today we are playing some Yona mid. I was actually going to play Yasuo, but uh into Zoey, but it was banned, so I'm playing Yon instead. Uh this matchup was one in the past that was pretty frustrating when Yona was definitely stronger than he is now cuz I don't remember when the nerfs were, but Yona uh kind of takes a while to scale to become strong. And uh the reason why this matchup previously was like super good for Yona or at least like frustrating for Zoe is because your E can remove her sleep so easily. Um and being able to remove her sleep is very powerful because being able to remove it uh kind of sets her up to only be able to damage you with like Q's and auto attacks. But Yona is that on your third Q. Uh it does have like a also a window of after using the first one you do have to hit again with a Q. You have a dash forwards. You are a auto attack crit champion just like Yasawo. And uh you get double the crit chance. Um when you build crit items, which makes uh building crit very very strong, of course. Oo, you almost landed that on me, but you didn't. Uh I do have bone plating, so at least if you hit me here, it won't be the worst trade ever. titre: Pandemonium, Arena & More | Dev Update - League of Legends
transcription:
Hey, everyone. I'm Andrei. Also known as Meddler. Paul. That's your line. Thank you Andrei. I'm Paul, also known as Pabro. Today is March 26th, and we're here to talk to you a bit
about what you can expect in Season 2, as well as a few other more
general updates. Quick spoiler warning. If you haven't seen the Season 2 Act 1
gameplay trailer that went live earlier today, we’ll pause for a few seconds
so you can check that out. Okay. Okay, welcome to Season 2: Pandemonium. In this season,
we're moving to the outskirts of Damacia, where we'll be following Vayne as she does
what she does best: hunts down demons. And along the way, we'll also learn
a little bit more about her as she deals with a few demons from her past
that have come to haunt her. Make sure you keep an eye out for comics on Vayne coming out later
this season for more details. All right, now let's jump into some other things
you can expect to see in Season 2 this year. Starting off, we're going with the shorter season for this one,
lasting a total of six patches instead of the usual eight to make room
for a longer season later this year. As a result, we've also got Battle Pass
changes to make it quicker to complete. So the amount of time it takes relative to
the length of the season should be roughly the same. We're also going to be trying
something different with the Seasonal Battle
Pass skins as well. Many of you have asked
that we make them direct purchase, so we're going to be doing
just that. In this season, the non-Prestige Battle Pass
skins will be available in the store and taking their place on the Battle
Pass will be Fiendish Mystery Skin Loot Orbs that will drop a random demon-
themed Epic skin instead. On the topic of skins, in the last Update,
we heard from quite a few of you that wanted to hear some more
about upcoming skins. So we're going to do just that,
starting with a short Prestige list. Shaco and LeBlanc are coming in Season 2
and Veigar's coming early next season. Now, in terms of non-Prestige skins,
here's a sneak peek at some of the other skins coming out this season. Now as you saw earlier in the video
we've got a new skin coming for Annie plus a few other champs
that we're calling Pandemonium. And for those of you who haven't gotten a
skin for their champion in quite a while, we've got you covered as well with Rain Shepherd Ivern and
PROJECT: Quinn coming soon. And last
but certainly not least, are some very tasteful skins we spent
a lot of time cooking up for you. Hope you're egg-cited like me.
(Kill me.) So lettuce take a look. Day Jobs—
That was great, Andrei, by the way. It's killing me. Day Jobs is making the return in Season 2. And this time
we're trying out a cooking theme. If you couldn't tell by our terrible puns.
His terrible puns. Okay. Thank you. Making her debut, as well,
is Breadsticks Irelia Don't let her get on a roll. Otherwise you're going to say—
Dough no! Inspired by our delicious,
wonderful code, Spaghetti alla Vel'Koz, will also be serving
on the Rift later this season. And look, remember,
when you're playing League, you're family, You don't always get
along, but we hope you do. All right, switching gears a bit,
we want to talk a bit about the gameplay changes
that we have coming for Season 2. We have some follow up
work on the Season 1 gameplay changes, particularly role quests. Some of the most common topics
that we've seen were uncertainty around where in lane you got top or mid
lane role quest progress. And that certain champions
who preferred to proxy or roam, you know, like assassins or Singed, were struggling
to keep up with role quests as a result. So we've adjusted where you get
your progress for your role quest in lane and how forgiving it is. To be clear,
you'll still need to come to Lane some of the time to progress
on your role quest, but you won't be penalized for doing
the things your champions want to do. Additionally, we've adjusted the rewards
for top to provide more experience for teamfighting and swapped the mid lane
empowered recall for 6% bonus AD and AP. Our goal with these changes
is to better reward the variety of classes and champions
that get played in these roles, rather than mainly rewarding
split pushers in top lane or non-roaming champs in mid, and to provide
some scaling rewards for mid laners as well. Now, outside of role quests, we're investing in some longer
term work to expand build diversity for a lot of champions. This will show up in things like item
adjustments, new keystones, and better supporting alternative builds
to increase strategy and creativity. So think things like AP Ezreal
or ADC Kennen or attack speed Xin Zhao. One of the ways
you'll see this in Season 2 is the return of two runes, Deathfire
Touch and Stormraider's Stormsurge. Both keystones will be in the Sorcery tree
with Stormraider's Surge replacing Phase Rush. We also have a variety of item adjustments
to items including Dusk and Dawn, two new starting items,
new Omnivamp boots, and the removal of Trailblazer
and Opportunity. We also wanted to talk briefly about
the next new champion coming to League. They'll be coming out in Season 2 Act 2, so
we don't want to spoil too much right now. We do want to let you know, though,
that they are a AP mid lane assassin, and you should expect to hear more
about them in our next Dev Update. All right. We're going to pass things over to Eduardo and Mike to talk about some updates
we have planned for Arena. Hey everyone. I'm in the Eduardo "Riot Cadmus" Cortejoso. And I'm Mike "RogueFool" Breese,
lead designer on Arena. And we're here to talk to you about Arena. We've got a lot of information
to share with you, so we're just going to go straight
into it, starting with events. Events are new ways to experience Arena, with an added twist. For this season, we'll be introducing
three events starting in patch 26.10. Our first event is going to be three
by six, aka six teams of three. Three players on each team. No longer
do you need to leave a friend behind to enjoy Arena, as long as you don't
have more than two friends. Anyway, having three players on each team
should help create a wider variety of team comps and allow more champions
of each type to find success in the mode. Meanwhile, having fewer teams comes
with the benefit of allowing you to learn other teams play styles and matchups
when you go back to rematch them again and keep that in mind. Three by six will stay on
for three patches, and after that we'll be switching to a long time
community ask: bravery. Are you tired of going up against people playing Yuumi / Alistar or Zyra and Shaco? Well, this is your time to shine. This is something many of you have
been asking for and we're here to deliver. Everyone in the lobby will automatically
be opted into playing bravery. if you really don't want to leave it
to good old fashioned chance, you'll still be able to pick from
a few random crowd favorites every game. This should help reward players
that are able to play flexibly and adapt to any situation
or champion that they get. Finally, our last event for
the season is Swift Arena. In this event, we’re looking to make games of Arena faster by limiting
the number of teams down to four. This means you'll have more reps
against each team so you can take advantage
of knowing their play styles, and getting more games
if you're running short on time. And then after that... Well,
we haven't decided on what happens after. We're planning on using learnings
from these different events to better understand what elements
of Arena really resonate with all of you. In the meanwhile,
we think events are something that will keep the game
fresh and exciting throughout the season. So all right, that's everything on events,
but we have a bit more in store for Arena this season. Next up we have a brand new map
called the Petricite Grove. The Petricite Grove is on the outskirts of Demacia, with large walls
and plenty of places to hide. But be careful. There are also mining bombs used to mine
petricite scattered throughout the map. When struck, bombs will roll before
exploding, damaging opponents and plants in the area. If you strike a bomb
with an immobilizing effect, they'll explode instantly, allowing you
to blast your opponents to pieces. The next new feature we have coming to
the arena is Augment Levels. Augment levels
let you supercharge your favorite Augments by making them stronger
and adding some new effects like Fan the Hammer, for example,
firing more missiles. Once you've picked two Augments to level
up, you can use your future choices to upgrade them
instead of just adding new ones. We really wanted to empower players
to lean into the Augments that they have the most fun with,
and raise their high rolls to new heights. And speaking of high rolls, well,
you can still only have four Augments most of the time, Augments will be offered
much more frequently this season, allowing you to select a new Augment level
an existing one, replace one with one of a higher tier,
and in Round Eight's new crafting round, you can fine-tune your build by choosing
to gain an extra Augment slot, sacrificing one of your Augments to power up the rest. And last but not least, what would be an update to Arena
without Augments and Guests of Honor, we have over 20 new reworked Guests
of Honor coming up throughout the season. Some of these will include Nocturne,
who will create dark zones around the map, and Shaco, who will create chaos
and make all your Augment rolls random tiers. We won't be listing to the rest here, but you can expect a few more demon-
themed guests to make an appearance. As for Augments,
we've got over 30 new ones, some coming from ARAM: Mayhem
and some brand new to League. All right, that's it for Arena's
updates this time around. Be sure to check out all the changes
we have talked about and a few that we didn't. And we'll see you in a Petricite Grove. Hello again gamers I'm DashiJador. And I'm Riot CallMeKT. WASD has reached a big milestone
that we're excited to share with you all. WASD will be available in Ranked
starting in patch 26.9, coming out with Season 2. First, we want to give a big
thank you to the hundreds of thousands of players who tested WASD
at various points of development. Your feedback was instrumental
in getting what we call Ranked ready. But Kellye,
what do we mean by "Ranked ready?" Well, when we began rolling out WASD,
we did so in unranked queues first so that we could address
any bugs or unintended interactions before LP was on the line. And of course, we wanted to ensure
that a whole new control scheme would be balanced
both in perception and in practice. After extensive testing, analysis,
and adjustments, we've gotten to the point where WASD has reached a similar performance
level as Point and Click controls. There's still a small delta in the win
rates between the two control schemes, with Point and Click
having a minor advantage. We expect that difference will decrease
over time as players gain more mastery with WASD, so we'll continue
to monitor this stat in the future. We've also been working on an
exciting new feature for all players, regardless of which control scheme
you want to use: the ability to set up champions specific keybinds. Starting tomorrow, you can configure
different keybinds for all of your mains. I suggest hopping into the Practice Tool so that you have plenty of time to explore
the new options before minions spawn. And last
but not least, we've been recently improving several of our accessibility
options for all of these control schemes. These changes include expanded keybind
support, enabling remapping of your mouse, as well as increased cursor size,
just to name a few. For those interested, more detailed
information on everything we've covered
can be found in a new dev blog coming out today. We first set out on this journey
when we discovered that new players sometimes struggle with League's traditional point and click control scheme. With WASD officially becoming an option
in all of League of Legends modes we hope that everyone can enjoy League with whichever control scheme
they find most intuitive. Thanks so much. We'll see you on the Rift. Alright, we also wanted to give
you a heads up on something we've been working on. A really frustrating experience in League is having your game ruined
by someone just straight inting. And while you can report them
and they will get punished, you still have your time wasted, stuck
in a game you had no chance of winning because someone decided to troll. Starting in a few patches,
you'll be given an option to vote to end the game early
when game ruining behavior is detected. This will function
much like when there's an AFK teammate. Once the system detects that a player
is intentionally feeding, you know, doing something like selling all their items
or running it down or whatever, the other players on their team
will be offered a surrender vote. If that vote,
which does not include the inting players, goes through
the game will be ended then and there. Players on the allied
team will be LP neutral. Players on the enemy team will receive
full LP as though they had won. And the offending player and, if they have one,
their premade party will all lose LP. And, of course, the offending player
will also receive a punishment. Another update we wanted to share
is that we're going to begin rolling out a new Discord integration
with the League client starting with a beta in the U.S., Canada,
and Brazil. In short, you'll be able to link
your Discord and Riot accounts to easily invite your Discord friends
to in-game parties, see who's playing, and join League lobbies
via Party Links. And when we say easily,
we mean literally from your friends list. No alt tabbing necessary. Party Links allow you to copy
and paste a link into your Discord server, socials, or stream
to invite all your friends to join. So no more manually inviting one by one. Players in the U.S., Canada, and Brazil will be able to connect your Riot account
to Discord starting tomorrow. We'll continue to test
and refine over this beta and plan on expanding
to additional regions in a few patches. To be clear, this is different from the in-game Team
Voice that we talked about last month. We don't have any news on that right now,
but if you're curious about how we're approaching Team Voice, check out the dev blog
we recently released. All right. So that's pretty much all of the major
updates we've got for you today. But before we go, we do have a few smaller things that we wanted
to give you all a heads up about. First off,
some of the League team will be showing up at Gamescom in Brazil at the end of April. So do be sure to stop by and say hi. Next up, Your Shop is coming back
on May 5th, and the Blue Essence Emporium will be coming out
the following week on May 13th. And something we forgot to mention when it went live is that custom games
are now supported for all live game modes, including things like Mayhem and Arena
that weren't previously supported. All right, so one last thing before we go. We have some content from all of you
that we wanted to highlight. So let's take a look. First up, we have this piece
from a Brazilian artist for the Fan Fest at First Stand. Featuring
the newly reworked Shyvana. And we have this beautiful rendered
image of Bilgewater lit up by a wonderful item I believe is
called a ward. I don't know, I don't see those in my games as much as I should. Yeah, that's a you problem. And then we have this fan art of Firecracker Seraphine. I like the colors here. Next, we've got a number of cosplayers
starting off with this fantastic Aurora cosplay. And this awesome Lux,
look at the detail in the armor. It's pretty sick. I also love the Galio in the background
there. Yeah. Good posing. And look at this really cool Spirit
Blossom Ahri cosplay. Still one of my favorite
skin lines. It's really cool.

titre: Season 1 2026 Theme, Gameplay & More | Dev Update - League of Legends
transcription:
Hey everyone. I’m Paul aka Pabro. And I’m Andrei, also known as Meddler. Today is October 30th,
which is a bit earlier than usual because we’re flying to Worlds soon and we wanted to film this before we left. This is our last Dev Update of the year, and before we get into some of the things
you’ll see in Season One 2026, we wanted to thank you all for being on
the journey with us this year. As always, we wanted to show 
some love for your passion,  for keeping us honest, and for making working on 
this game so worth it. Now, in January, we’ll be back 
to talk more about Season One, as well as a little bit of what to expect
for the rest of 2026 overall. But for now, let’s get into it. We learned a lot this year about Seasons. Many of you liked what came 
with the themes: the map art updates, 
metagames, new champions, and champ updates 
overall, for example. But we also saw that
if we leaned too hard into a region it could feel very one-note,
especially when it comes to skins. And as it turns out, there is 
TOO MUCH of a thing like Noxus. - Lotta red and black.
- Yeah, yeah. So, we’ll continue to find the right
balance for thematic Seasons moving forwards. Speaking of which, it’s time 
to talk about Season One of 2026. For this, we wanted 
a contrast from 2025, y’know, with its ancient danger, a Darkin, and quite a bit 
of scheming throughout. So next year we want to kick things off
with themes of unity, hope, and justice... with a drop of righteous judgment
thrown in there for good measure. So, that means petricite, gold,
and blue accents on the map, as well as some Demacia-themed skins,
including some champions reimagined as Demacians. With plenty of non-Demacia
skins for some variety. That also means that 
the yearly cinematic in January will feature Demacian champions. We’re going back to old school Demacia this time. Back in time to what some might consider
the golden age of Demacia. When it was a beacon of hope
and a bastion of justice. Hey Andrei, ummm, 
are we forgetting something? I don't think so. You sure? Nah, yeah. Let's keep going. I don't want this to drag on... Anyway, we have a range 
of Summoner’s Rift changes that the team will talk about in a moment. And this year, we’ll also talk 
about some updates to Swiftplay. With that, we’re going to hand it over
to Riot Endstep and Phreak who will walk you through some of the
Summoner’s Rift changes coming in January. Hey everyone I’m Riot Endstep. And I’m Phreak. And we’re here to talk to you about the Summoner’s Rift gameplay 
changes coming in January. Over the last few years, League has been largely about 
teamfighting over neutral objectives: fighting around grubs, dragons, or that vision ward you just placed 
around grubs or dragons. And, while we know 5v5s make for 
some of the highest highs,  we wanna swing back towards 
a state where more strategies like splitting or sieging 
are more viable. So we’re making some changes
to major neutral objectives, towers, and minions 
in order to do just that. Additionally, we’re
making adjustments to roles so everyone has an appropriate impact
on the outcome of each game because right now, 
some lanes are much better at turning a winning lane 
into a winning game. We also have some 
de-light-ful improvements. So, let’s get started. The first thing you’ll notice in January
is that the game starts faster. We want to reduce downtime
after you load in,  so minions and basic jungle camps first spawn 35 seconds earlier 
than they do today. You'll still be able
to pull off early invades, but you'll have to be much more decisive
when you load-in. We’re also making some changes to vision. It’s an incredibly powerful
tool in League, but right now it feels monopolized
by supports and sometimes by junglers. We want to make effectively warding
the map easier to learn for everyone and give more opportunities and power
to wards dropped by pushing champions, especially mid and top laners
who are side-laning later in the game. So we’re adding Faelights: locations that grant temporary 
bonus vision to wards,  with unique bonus vision areas 
for each spot. Some Faelight rings will be available
at the beginning of each game, while others reveal themselves
when the Elemental Rift transforms, particularly those that would
otherwise disrupt early game jungle. Okay, so, on to the objectives. Atakhan was one of the biggest 
additions to the Rift this year. It was a pretty cool, unique
objective to fight over. That said, he contributed to 
objective fatigue, which many of you cited 
as an issue and we agree. We wanted to alleviate this over time, we had removed the second form of Atakhan, we removed the second set of Grubs, but it really wasn’t enough. So, just like Zaahen did, we’re cutting Atakhan out of the game. That includes Blood Roses 
and the Petals with them. That said, let us know what you think
about limited time gameplay changes and if they’re something you’d
like to see more of. But back to Atakhan,
his removal also means  that Baron will go back to 
his old 20 minute spawn time. Welcome back, king. Additionally, we're making 
all epic monsters more durable, especially late-game dragons
as you get closer to dragon soul. That should make it
so that it’s easier for teams on the backfoot to contest dragons
and other epic monsters, or trade something meaningful
on the other side of the map. Finally, we’re also removing
Feats of Strength from the game. We liked that they pushed teams 
to progress the game and provided goals to rally around, but we think we can create better
gameplay systems that allow for players to have more individual agency
over their games. With Feats getting the boot, we’re adding back some gold rewards 
for first blood and first turret. Another way we’re looking to improve
sieging is with a new mechanic called Crystalline Overgrowth, which is kinda
like a universal Demolish effect. Crystals will build up on towers 
over several minutes, and when hit, 
they are consumed to deal an extra burst of damage
based on how much buildup has accumulated. This should help enable pushing windows
in more situations,  even if you're behind or 
don't have the highest DPS. Demolish will be sticking around, but will be simplified
to a good ol’ three-hit-passive. - We love three hit passives.
- Yes we do. Turret plates will now stay 
on outer turrets permanently, and will be added to the inner 
and inhibitor towers as well. We’re doing this to create long-term,
piecemeal rewards for pushing; no matter what state the turret’s in,  you’re likely to mine some gold 
by taking aggressive actions. We’re not adding more gold to the map
with these changes, instead, we’re gonna be 
basically spreading out the current gold for killing a tower and splitting it across the plates. To further facilitate pushing, we’re ramping up minion respawn rates 
in the late game to give more frequent
access to sieging turrets. We’re also adjusting late-game Homeguards to swiftly take you to your 
most pushed minion wave in each lane. And finally, Nexus turrets
will respawn with partial health missing. We like that the respawn allows a team
to hold the base for a while, and not have to worry about 
getting backdoored by a Twitch, but with a full health respawn,
it meant that it was honestly incorrect for a sieging team to finish killing 
the turret sometimes. Now for our biggest change
for next year: role quests. When you load into the game 
in January, top, bot, and mid lane will now have their own
quests that reward unique power similar to the jungle pet evolutions, 
and the support item upgrades. You’ll progress these new quests
by killing minions, champions, plates, towers, 
or epic objectives. All the things you’d normally 
be doing in a game. You’ll progress even faster
by doing those in your assigned lane. Your quest is decided by which role 
you’ve been assigned in the lobby, so if you and a friend (or a stranger)
wanna change roles, make sure you do an official trade in champ select. One of our goals with these role
quests is to buff top and bot carry relative to the other roles, 
as they’re currently the two weakest. And, as a result, the quests rewards 
are gonna be comparatively stronger for them. Now let’s get into the rewards which will
be granted in a new role-quest slot. Historically, top laners
haven't always felt like they have the agency
over the games that they deserved, even if they won their lane, and that’s something we would 
like to change this season. Upon quest completion, top laners will receive pretty much 
Summoner Teleport for free, on top of the two summoners
they already chose. Now if you had taken Teleport
in pre game instead, that spell gets a lot stronger, giving a very, very large shield 
when you use it. In addition to getting a 
functionally free Summoner Spell, top laners will also get
a bunch of bonus XP up front, more XP from future sources, and an increased level cap. Now, to mid lane. A huge variety of champions
can be played there, so we wanted this reward to be good  whether you’re a mage, assassin, 
or both, like Malphite mid. And upon completing the quest, mid laners will receive a free tier 3 boot upgrade like the ones you could buy with Feats,  as well as a faster recall. Rounding it out for new quests, in bot lane, we’re gonna lean even harder into the “carry” part of the role, so while top laners get bonus XP, bot laners are getting a whole bunch of extra gold. When completing their quest,  they get a lump sum of gold, increased gold from minion kills, 
champ kills, and champion assists for 
the rest of the game. And you might be asking, well then what do I do with all this
gold if I hit item cap? Don't worry, the item cap is going up. Your seventh item slot will hold
your boots permanently, allowing you to get six full legendary items for extra hard late game scaling. Jungle and support
already have quests and rewards, but we still wanted to take this
opportunity to make some changes to them. Jungle is, by far, the strongest role 
in the game right now especially when it comes to 
early map influence. So we’re slightly lowering 
the speed of early game clears to give lane match ups a little bit
more time to play out uninterrupted. We’re also making it harder
for non-junglers to steal objectives by increasing their resists
and making Smite deal more damage. Their role quest is still 
getting pet upgrades  and PVP Smite like before, and they’re now getting increased gold 
and XP from large monsters when the quest is done, as well as bonus movement speed 
in the jungle. Similar to jungle, we think support 
is already plenty powerful and has a very functional role quest, so we didn’t want to directly increase their power. With that in mind,  we wanted to give them
quality of life oriented upgrades, so we’re giving them a new slot
exclusively for control wards,  discounts on control wards, and a little bit more gold over time so they can keep pace 
a little better with other roles. Overall we want these quests to represent
meaningful power spikes in a way that leans into the 
unique strengths of each role. This also allows us to delete the 
Lane Swap Detected mechanic we added last year to combat
Pro laneswapping. We’ll be keeping a close eye 
on how quests land, so please let us know 
your thoughts next year! But role quests aren’t
all we have planned to make your champions feel more satisfying. We’re gonna bring back a few items
to the Rift for each class to give them some shiny new toys, including bringing back a few 
familiar favorites like Hextech Gunblade. For example,  we’re adding the new Scepter of Bonking 
for AP fighters, which will empower your Spellblade bonk 
to double your on-hit effects. ...That’s not the final name is it? Uhhhh...  I would like it but no,  we’re probably gonna give it 
something in-canon. I do love bonking. Well, anyway. Given none of these names are final, if you’re an AD fighter without 
a good way to get Omnivamp, you can grab Blood Sphere which will give you some Omnivamp and also Ability Haste scaling with your AD. Or if you play an AD carry
who’s really good at auto spacing, you can pick up Snowbow
for extra range on takedown, plus damage amp based on
how far away you are from your opponents. We took a hard look at 
which sub-classes didn’t feel like they were getting enough support
from the current item system and worked to fill those spaces, so we’re hoping many champions
have at least one of the 9 new items that they can get excited about! Speaking of, we’re also adding 
crit damage back to 200%. All right. We know that was a lot to cover. We’re excited to hear
what you all think of these changes as the new Season kicks off. And in case you’re looking for even more 
details or insights on these changes  make sure to check out
our dev blog that’s live right now. That’s it from us on the 
Summoner’s Rift team, and so we’ll throw it back
to Pabro and Meddler, and we’ll see YOU on the Rift! Okay, let’s talk about Ranked. This year, we’ve been making improvements
to shorten queue times and make your matches more fair through 
improvements to inting and griefing detection. And, more recently,
we ran a test in late September that reduced queue times by 40% at all
ranks in Ranked in EU West and OCE. We want to double down on that work
and make 2026 the best time yet to climb. So, next year we’ll be also tackling 
2 common frustrations: mismatched teams and long queues. Something we want to address is visual rank disparity in lobbies as well. When players start their climbs 
late after a reset, you may see a Bronze player
in an otherwise Gold game, which can particularly feel unfair. When in reality, 
that player was Gold last Season, but they haven’t gotten back to that Rank yet because they’ve had less time to play. So we’re adding a climb indicator
to loading screens when a player’s
MMR is higher than their visible rank. This will let other players
know that you’re climbing quickly, and your displayed rank
hasn’t caught up yet. We’re also making changes to how 
Flex and Solo/Duo MMR are calculated. A common pain point is someone
who is Silver in Flex and, say, Diamond in Solo/Duo,
just stomping a bunch of games, when, in reality, they’re truly 
Diamond in both ladders. So this Season, if your Solo/Duo MMR
is much higher than your Flex MMR, the system should bring your Flex
rank a lot closer to your Solo/Duo one. And that should mean Flex matchups will feel
a fair bit more balanced moving forwards. Next, we’re making changes to autofill. The reality of League is that certain roles are substantially more popular than others. And, having an autofilled-jungler versus
a jungle main can feel like an auto-loss. This means that quite often people
either dodge to avoid it, or hold your lobby hostage
in order to get you to dodge. When nobody likes queue times,
this is an awful experience. So, we have a number of changes
to make autofill fairer, and to reward you for sticking 
with the role you’re assigned when you do get autofilled. First, if someone on your team 
is autofilled, the system will try to match
autofilled players by role, so an autofilled jungler vs.
another autofilled jungler, for example. If it can’t match by roles,
it will then of course fall back and try to ensure the same number
of autofilled players on each team. We do know, though, that 
even with that balance, autofill games can still feel discouraging  when you play your best 
in a role you didn’t choose. That’s why we’re adding a new system
called “Aegis of Valor.” If you’re autofilled and earn 
a mastery score of C or greater, the Aegis will trigger. You won’t lose any LP on a loss,
or you'll earn double LP for a win. So for those of you
who main less popular roles, and therefore seldom get autofilled…
first of all, thanks! We haven’t forgotten about you. You'll still occasionally receive
the Aegis benefit  at about the same rate as other players, because we want to reward the role heroes
who keep the queue healthy and moving. Finally, we know how frustrating it is
to be constantly returned to the queue because you get chain dodges 
lobby after lobby. So, we’ve made a few updates. First, after a positive response
to our pilot test, we’re removing the ability to ban
a hovered ally champion, taking away an opportunity for a teammate  to tilt off the planet before 
the match even starts. And then second, dodging a lobby will no longer allow 
players to escape autofill, it’ll just get carried over to the next lobby. We think that the Aegis of Valor should provide a solid reward
for sticking around and trying. So I mean, come on,
it is double LP for a win, after all. And, at Master and above, dodging
an autofill will count as a full loss. We know that this is a pretty big change from how we’ve dealt
with dodging in the past, and we believe this will lead
to higher quality and faster games. Speaking of speeding things up, we’re also speeding up Champ Select, by cutting down on timers and animations, which should save about half a minute. - I see what you did there. 
- Yeah, I did it. And if that wasn’t enough, we’ve
got one last Ranked change. We’re adding back Duo Queue
to higher ranks, including Challenger, in most regions. For so many people, 
League is better with friends. And in the past, 
we disabled duoing at Apex tier ranks because it so often led to Rank manipulation. But, because of the improvements
we mentioned earlier, as well as the work on boosting
and Ranked manipulation detection, we are confident that we can bring back
high MMR duoing without it being so abused. So call up your friends; it’s duo time. Switching gears, 
let’s talk about Swiftplay. Swiftplay is pretty popular, and we think there’s a lot 
of room for further improvement. And we want Swiftplay to be that place
where you can get in a quick game, where you and/or some friends
can practice new champions, or where new players can come and
learn and get their footing on the Rift. That means Swiftplay should still be
somewhat competitive, while providing quicker queues, 
faster games, and serving both new and existing players. So we’re rolling out some changes
that should address the overall pacing. Touching objectives, laning,
and the jungle. To start with, we’re bringing over 
a lot of the changes to SR generally that were talked about earlier
in this video, except for Role Quests. Now, for Swiftplay-specific changes though,
the first thing we’re tackling is game pacing. Swiftplay games are actually a bit long, and the general consensus is
there isn’t quite enough action. So things like long death timers, focusing on CSing in lane, and objective overload all take away
from the promise of Swiftplay. So we want to get you to 
the action faster and more often. Now, while some of the Season One 
changes will help address this, you’ll also start Swiftplay at level 3 
with some extra gold,  similar to ARAM. We’re also lowering death timers 
across the entire game  so you’re getting back 
into the action quicker. And, in order to keep the game 
from stalling out, we’ve looked for ways for you to capitalize
on the shorter death windows. So we’re increasing the 
minion spawn frequency, putting cannon minions in 
every wave after the third, and introducing a new mechanic 
that we’re calling “Minion Frenzy.” If you kill an enemy champion 
near your minions, they’ll be inspired by your actions. They’ll get enraged, and attack faster 
and deal more damage. We’re also gonna be making 
some adjustments to tower durability and making Sudden Death start
a little bit earlier. And thus... Swift! Outside of pacing, we also want
to decrease objective overload. While the removal of Atakhan helps, we’re also gonna be removing the Grubs, and Rift Herald. Baron and Elder Dragon
will also be spawning earlier, with Elder spawning at 15 minutes, regardless of whether the 
Dragon Soul has been claimed. The game-ending power of these buffs
being accessible much sooner should help in closing games a bit
faster than what we’re currently seeing. And with Elder Dragon moving up, the Elemental Rift will transform 
after one dragon,  and you’ll get the Soul after two. Finally, rounding out these changes 
for Swiftplay: Jungle. Now, currently, if you’re not 
a seasoned jungle main it's... it can actually be very 
overwhelming in Swiftplay. These broader pacing changes 
around neutral objectives should make it feel much more comfortable and like you’re not being swarmed 
with objectives all the time. Now, we know this is a 
ton of information and changes but the goal here is for Swiftplay
to feel swifter,  without it being so dense 
that it’s overwhelming. We have a dev blog coming out today
that will cover all of these changes plus a lot more detail 
behind the specifics. So if you want to learn more, go for it. Hi everyone! I’m Dashijador, product lead.  Back again to chat about WASD, 
this time with... The tech lead for WASD, 
I’m Riot CallMeKT. Now that Worlds is over and we’re
getting closer to the end of the year, we want to check back in
on the wild world of WASD. We’ve been on the PBE 
for a few months now, and in that time, we’ve caught and 
squashed over 100 bugs. All thanks to the feedback
we received from all of you creative, maybe a little unhinged PBE players
who’ve been helping us find, identify and crush
all of these pesky bugs. An extra special thanks to the players who were able to retrigger
the notorious Nunu bug. Even beyond bugs, PBE players have given
us great recommendations and requests! One Redditor recommended
that we add an option to do single-button input to move at a 45-degree angle 
up and down the lane. It sounded pretty wild at first, but we found through our internal testing that it actually saved a lot of finger pain. So it will be available as an accessibility option
for WASD players going forward. Since our debut on the PBE, we’ve been continuously updating 
all the different facets of WASD. We’ve made improvements
to navigating around minions, wall sliding, the new dynamic locked 
camera, and more. One of these features is a new
auto-attack system for WASD. With practice and dedication, you’ll
be able to weave attacks and movement, matching the experience in Point
and Click to reach your optimum DPS. Our goal is to keep WASD as an alternative
control scheme at all levels of play, not just for brand new players. We will be keeping an eye on feedback as well as our internal data to ensure it
remains an equitable experience. We’ve also been updating our 
Dynamic Locked camera. We’ve added the highly-requested ability
to tune the camera’s speed to your liking. Along with these settings,
we heard your feedback on wanting a way to “Scout Ahead”
to check for ganks, look at other lanes, and more. So we’ve introduced a new feature appropriately named the 
“Scout Ahead” mode. When you’re in Dynamic Locked Camera, you can hold your middle mouse button to freely move your camera, and you’ll snap back on release. These features, alongside
all of the bugfixes, will be available when WASD goes live tomorrow in all queues
except Ranked and Normal Draft games. We are choosing to keep WASD
out of draft modes for now, as we want to make sure you can have
a smooth process for your Ranked climb. Really, tomorrow? Welllll,
WASD will be live in all unranked queues, except Normal Draft, in Patch 25.24. Which for many of you, 
should be tomorrow! We saw your requests and questions
about other features we’ve promised, like champion-specific keybinds and enabling many of these new features 
for Point and Click players. We’re still working on them, 
and much more to come. We're at the point where we want to focus on getting 
large amounts of player data in order to drive our development further. So, we're opening up the feedback channels
to our much more global audience. Now, we still don’t have an exact timeline
on when WASD will come to Ranked. We want to ship it when it’s ready, which means fair, balanced,
bug-free, and smooth to play. Please keep up the feedback and bug reports, there may still be a few 
stragglers out there. Only through our powers combined
can we find all of them! That’s it for us! Be sure to check back out on WASD
when it’s live, and send your bugs, feedback,
and best misplays! Thanks so much for listening, and we’ll
see you WASDing on the Rift! So how are your Pick’Ems? Not great. A bit too much hopium
when I filled them out. I know how it feels,
but uhhh... the hopium is feeling good for me. I think I'm actually feeling great. Really? - Totally.
- Uh-huh. Who's wearing the suit then? We... We don't know. Not gonna be me. I mean... We'll see. - Good luck. 
- Okay. Okay. We know that this was a long one
with a lot of info in it. And we’ll see you again in January,
as mentioned,  to talk a bit more about Season One, and a little bit about the rest of 2026. Don’t forget that Ranked will stay open
until the new Season, so go finish your climbs, have a happy and safe holiday season, and we’ll see you on the Winter Rift. - Thank you so much. 
- Bye.

even some high ranked players really don't check their mini map as much as they should so sure it's a useful skill to develop but just don't stress about it too much when you're first starting out focus on the basics first and mini map awareness is really going to come naturally with time anyway above the mini map you can see your team along with their Level Health mana and coold down of their ultimate that's their r displayed by the green orb above that you can check the respawn timers of dead enemy Champions then in the top right you have your own teams and the enemy teams kill count to useful information like when certain objectives are spawning but again more on that later don't stress yourself out with it just yet then to move or attack here it's all done through right clicking with your mouse and no you can't use WD to move so just get used to it you can even right click on the mini map to travel longer distances as well if that's your thing to unlock your camera you want to press Y and unbind this so you don't accidentally lock your camera then to move your camera drag your cursor to the top bottom or sides of your screen you can even left click on the mini map to look at specific areas if you so choose if you find the camera movement a little bit too fast or too slow you can adjust the sensitivity under options game camera movement speed when you start a game on Summoner Rift your team of five players splits up across different parts of the map one player goes to the Middle Lane that's your mid laner one heads to the Top Lane yep that is your top laner two players team up and go to the bottom Lane these are your support and ADC which is short for attack damage carry and one player focuses on farming neutral camps located between lanes we call this the jungle and their role is well you guessed it called the jungler the spot you choose to play in actually helps decide what kind of champion you're going to pick and what your role is going to be inside of the game so let's start with mid lane here it's the most popular Lane and many players say it's the most important role in the game and I was also told to say some dude called Faker plays there or something like that okay mid puts you right in the center of everything that happens which means you can get to any part of that map pretty quickly if you enjoy playing Mages or caster or even assassins that hunt for kills then this is likely going to be the lane for you casters and mages Thrive here as mid is the shortest Lane of all three and mages in League while dishing out tons of damage do tend to be squishy and immobile meaning being able to play close to a defensive structure at all times is going to be a big reason for why they ended up primarily being played in mid and as for Assassins well asides from preing on them squishy Mages being in the center of the map means they can quickly sneak into other lanes to hunt for kills on enemy Champions so moving on to Top Lane this is where you're typically going to find two types of Champions Fighters and tanks see unlike mid top is a much longer Lane meaning in order to farm minions you're going to be much further away from the safety net of your turret turrets a little bit later on now for this reason you need to be a champion that's very self-sufficient and can hold their own either in the form of a fighter which is typically a melee Champion that does a ton of damage while still being pretty tough to kill but with the drawback of being easily kited or a tank which are champions built to soak up damage play on the front line and protect their team through various crowd control like stuns slows and knockups now some of them can even taunt giving you the true tank experience since Top Lane is usually the furthest away from all the action happening on the rest of the map it's perfect for players who love going head-to-head with their opponent in a mostly 1V one setting lastly there is bot Lane now if you like playing with a partner then this is going to be the lane for you the meta in bot Lane revolves around having one carry champion and is support playing together to win the lane 2v2 as the carry or the ADC your job is to well carry these types of Champions start off pretty weak in the early game and then they get stronger as the game progresses meaning that you're really going to need a certain level of patience to deal with these characters typically adcs end up providing the main source of consistent damage for their team you can honestly consider this role as the main character you're the playmaker the damage dealer and All Eyes eventually end up being on you to carry the game and well I'm sure at some point all the opposing enemy Champions are going to end up being on you too but just like most of the players this roll attracts you really need a babysitter this is where your support comes in as the support You Walk So Others May run it's your primary job to go bot Lane with your ADC to help them Farm no not by taking the minions yourself but by watching your ADC while they attempt to support Champions typically offer more in the way of utility than they do in the way of damage and while it may seem boring not getting much gold or much exp supports typically make up for it by having some of the most impactful abilities in the entire game of League of Legends with most Champions filling one of two roles either as an engage support who looks to set up their team with powerful stuns knockups or even grabs or as a more conventional Support called enchanter enchanters focus on keeping their team alive and buffing their damage through various Shields and various he heals if you ever played healers in other games enchanters should really feel very similar
